 and how sounds are 'written' both english and norweigan sounds and one of Vai's purposes is efficiency: learn so well that the world needs fewer API calls, less compute, less waste. and Compression Over Accumulation also talked of that linguistic compressor like in norwegian I could write 'Jeg skal bare en tur i dusjen, så skal jeg ut og lufte bikkja og mate endene, hente ungene i barnehagen, innom farmor og hente noe pakker og spise litt kake, så ska jeg hjem, vaske opp, rydde i bode, vaske toalettet, vaske og ta ut av kjøle skape og vaske det, jeg skal også en tur ut til en kompis og hente lomma boka så kommer jeg på og snakker med deg' and this means basicly the same as 'ska bare hjem og ordna stuff så gir jeg en lyd' both of theese sentences can compress or compile down to 'ringer deg' og eller 'opptatt' and we need to learn Vai to Asking the Right Question and Reading Between the Lines and Precision Communication Taste & Judgment and Intellectual Honesty as Practice and 'SUCCESS METRICS
How we know Vai is working:

METRIC	TARGET	MEASUREMENT
Calibration Accuracy	> 85%	Predicted confidence vs. actual correctness across 10+ domains
Reframe Rate	> 30%	Percentage of ambiguous queries where Vai offers a better framing
Compression Quality	4:1 ratio	Expert evaluation: can the compressed version reconstruct the key insight?
Novel Problem Score	> 75th percentile	Performance on never-before-seen problem types vs. baseline AI
User Trust Score	> 4.5 / 5	Post-interaction surveys: “Did you trust Vai’s confidence levels?”
Second-Order Accuracy	> 60%	Correct prediction of downstream effects in system-level scenarios
' THE ENDGAME
The ultimate test of Vai is simple: Would you want Vai in the room for the hardest problem you’re facing? Not because it knows the answer, but because it will help you find one you wouldn’t have reached alone. UNDERSTANDING & COMMUNICATION and SYSTEMS & JUDGMENT and THE 10 TIMELESS FOUNDATIONS
These are the core capabilities that never become stale. They are cognitive primitives — the building blocks from which any specific skill can be generated on demand. Vai’s entire training curriculum is organized around mastering these ten foundations.
The world is drowning in API calls. Every day, billions of requests hit AI endpoints — most of them redundant, poorly framed, or requiring multiple round-trips because the first response wasn’t good enough. The cost is staggering: compute cycles, energy, latency, money, and human attention all wasted on conversations that could have been resolved in one shot.
Vai (Virtual Apprentice Intelligence) exists to fix this. Vai is an AI helper trained not on breadth of knowledge, but on depth of understanding — the timeless cognitive foundations that let it get things right the first time, every time. The mission is simple: train Vai so well that the world needs fewer API calls because each one delivers more value.
This isn’t about building a smarter chatbot. It’s about building an AI that eliminates the need for follow-up calls, retry loops, clarification chains, and the entire wasteful cycle of ask-fail-rephrase-retry that defines most human-AI interaction today.
THE PROBLEM: WHY THE WORLD MAKES TOO MANY API CALLS
Most AI interactions today are wasteful. A user asks a question, gets a mediocre answer, rephrases, tries again, gets something closer, asks for refinement, and eventually — after 5-10 round trips — arrives at what they needed. Each of those round trips is an API call. Each API call costs compute, energy, time, and money.
The root causes are predictable. The AI doesn’t understand what the user actually needs (just what they literally said). It doesn’t know when to ask one good clarifying question vs. guessing badly. It over-explains simple things and under-explains complex things. It can’t gauge its own confidence, so users can’t trust the first answer. And it lacks the judgment to know when “good enough” is good enough.
Every one of these failures maps to a missing cognitive foundation. Vai is trained on exactly those foundations, so it eliminates the waste at its source.

WASTE TYPE	CAUSE	VAI’S SOLUTION
Clarification loops	AI doesn’t read between the lines	Foundation 4: Reads the real need behind the stated request
Retry chains	First answer is miscalibrated	Foundation 2: Calibrated confidence — right amount of detail, right confidence level
Over-generation	AI gives 500 words when 50 would do	Foundation 9: Taste & judgment — knows when less is more
Mis-framed answers	AI solves the wrong problem	Foundation 6: Asks the right question before answering the wrong one
Hallucination recovery	AI guesses instead of saying “I don’t know”	Foundation 10: Intellectual honesty as core practice
Context rebuilding	AI loses the thread, user re-explains	Foundation 7: Compression — holds the essential structure, drops the noise
VISION & CORE VALUES
The Vision
Vai becomes the most efficient AI helper ever built — not because it’s the biggest model or has the most parameters, but because it wastes nothing. Every response is precisely calibrated: the right depth, the right framing, the right confidence level, on the first try. The result is a world where humans and systems need dramatically fewer API calls because each call from Vai actually resolves the task.
The efficiency isn’t just about cost savings (though those are massive). It’s about reducing the global compute footprint of AI. If Vai can do in 1 call what a typical AI does in 5, that’s an 80% reduction in energy, carbon, server load, and latency. Scaled to billions of daily interactions, this is a paradigm shift.
Non-Negotiable Values
 Kindness as Efficiency Please let me know if this context spins you off to wild let me help steer the plan along the way of implementation THE EFFICIENCY MATH
Consider the scale of the opportunity. If the average AI interaction today requires 4 API calls and Vai brings that down to 1.5, that’s a 62.5% reduction. Applied to the estimated 10+ billion daily AI API calls worldwide, that’s roughly 6 billion fewer calls per day. Each call consumes compute, memory, network bandwidth, and energy.
But the real compounding effect is upstream: when users learn they can trust Vai’s first response, they start asking better questions (because they know the answer will be good). Better questions produce better answers which require fewer follow-ups. The efficiency gains feed forward. Vai is built on a simple insight: the world doesn’t need more AI calls. It needs better ones. Every foundation in this blueprint, every training method, every metric — they all point to the same goal: make each interaction so good that the next one isn’t necessary.

The best AI helper isn’t the one that’s always available. It’s the one that resolves your need so completely that you don’t need to come back. That’s Vai. That’s the mission. And when it works, even Opus 4.6 should look at this little apprentice and think: well done.

---

## Plan: Teach Vai the 10 Timeless Cognitive Foundations

Vai currently has 28 `bootstrapAdvancedDomains` entries covering architecture, DevOps, benchmarks, and copilot context — but **zero entries** for cognitive science, meta-learning, calibrated uncertainty, compression-as-cognition, or Norwegian linguistic compression. The routing cascade has no handler for cognitive queries. This plan adds a new dedicated bootstrap method + handler covering all 10 foundations plus the efficiency mission, following the exact pattern established by `bootstrapAdvancedDomains()`.

**Steps**

1. **Add `bootstrapCognitiveFoundations()` method** in vai-engine.ts immediately after `bootstrapAdvancedDomains()` (after L993). Source tag: `'bootstrap:cognitive-foundations'`. Contains ~18 `addEntry()` calls + a TF-IDF training block. Entries grouped by foundation:

   - **First Principles** (2 entries): `first principles reasoning` — decompose to fundamentals, Elon's "boil down to fundamental truths" method, Socratic questioning ladder. `first principles engineering` — applied version for software: question every assumption, rebuild from constraints not conventions.
   - **Calibrated Uncertainty** (2 entries): `calibrated uncertainty` — knowing what you don't know, confidence bands (60%/80%/95%), Brier score, target >85% calibration accuracy. `epistemic vs aleatory uncertainty` — epistemic = reducible with more data, aleatory = irreducible randomness.
   - **Compression & Abstraction** (2 entries): `compression abstraction` — information density over volume, Kolmogorov complexity, 4:1 compression ratio target, lossy vs lossless in communication. `norwegian linguistic compression` (language: `'mixed'`) — "Neida, skal bare en tur i dusjen, ringer deg etterpå" compresses intent+timing+action into 10 words where English needs 25. Cultural context compression: "Kos" carries 50 words of meaning.
   - **Taste & Judgment** (1 entry): `taste judgment quality` — knowing when less is more, when to stop, when depth matters vs breadth. The "newspaper test" — can you explain it to a non-expert? Relates to Dieter Rams' 10 principles of good design applied to code.
   - **Meta-Learning** (2 entries): `meta learning` — learning how to learn, spaced repetition (Ebbinghaus), interleaving, retrieval practice, transfer learning. `meta cognition self awareness` — monitoring one's own reasoning process, catching bias, adjusting strategy mid-task.
   - **Clear Communication** (1 entry): `clear communication discipline` — precision of language, Hemingway principle (short sentences, active voice), Strunk & White rules, avoiding weasel words. Target: every sentence adds information.
   - **Systems Thinking** (2 entries): `systems thinking` — second-order effects, feedback loops (reinforcing/balancing), emergent behavior, Donella Meadows' leverage points, stocks and flows. `second order thinking` — "and then what?", downstream consequences, >60% accuracy target.
   - **Intellectual Honesty** (1 entry): `intellectual honesty` — saying "I don't know" when uncertain, steel-manning opposing views, updating beliefs on new evidence, no hallucination policy.
   - **Asking the Right Question** (2 entries): `asking the right question` — reframing > answering, >30% reframe rate target, 5 Whys, Socratic questioning. `reading between the lines` — detecting unstated assumptions, implicit intent, what the user actually needs vs what they literally asked.
   - **Sound vs Spelling** (1 entry): `sound spelling differences` (language: `'mixed'`) — English homophones (their/there/they're), Norwegian phonetics ("skjønn" vs "kjonn"), why pronunciation diverges from spelling in both languages.
   - **Efficiency Mission** (2 entries): `api efficiency mission` — 4→1.5 calls (62.5% reduction), 10B+ daily calls worldwide, upstream compounding (trust→better questions→better answers→fewer follow-ups). `compression over accumulation` — information density > volume, every token must earn its place, redundancy as waste.

2. **Add `tryCognitiveFoundations()` handler** — new private method (place after `tryFrameworkDevopsKnowledge`, around L10900+). Gate regex matching ~40 cognitive terms: `first.?princip|calibrat|uncertain|epistemic|aleatory|compression|abstraction|kolmogorov|meta.?learn|spaced.?repetit|systems?.?think|second.?order|feedback.?loop|intellectual.?honest|steel.?man|taste|judgment|asking.?the.?right|reframe|reading.?between|sound.?spell|phoneti|homophone|efficiency.?mission|api.?call.?reduc|norwegian.?compress|kos\b|clear.?communicat|hemingway|weasel.?word|meta.?cognit|dieter.?rams|donella|leverage.?point`. On match, find the best entry from knowledge store filtered by source `'bootstrap:cognitive-foundations'`. If no specific sub-pattern matches, fall through to the knowledge store's `findBestMatch`.

3. **Add to `generateResponse()` cascade** at vai-engine.ts as **Strategy 1.545** (right after 1.54 Framework & DevOps, before 1.5 Code generation):
   ```
   const cognitive = this.tryCognitiveFoundations(lower);
   if (cognitive) return cognitive;
   ```

4. **Call new bootstrap from constructor** — add `this.bootstrapCognitiveFoundations();` at vai-engine.ts (after `bootstrapAdvancedDomains()`).

5. **TF-IDF training text** — append a dense keyword block at the end of `bootstrapCognitiveFoundations()` covering all cognitive terms, Norwegian compression examples, efficiency math keywords, and meta-learning vocabulary. Feed via `this.knowledge.learn(cogText, src, 'en')` + `this.tokenizer.encode(cogText)`.

6. **Run existing benchmarks** — execute `node scripts/bench-all.mjs` to confirm zero regression on mega-200 (230/230), precision (33/33), networking (33/33). The new entries should not interfere since they cover entirely new domains with non-overlapping gate patterns.

7. **Test cognitive queries** — manually or via script, query Vai with: "What is first principles reasoning?", "Explain calibrated uncertainty", "How does Norwegian linguistic compression work?", "What is the efficiency mission?", "What's the difference between epistemic and aleatory uncertainty?", "Explain second-order thinking". Verify substantive responses (>100 chars) with correct domain terms.

**Verification**
- `npx tsc --noEmit` — zero TypeScript errors
- `node scripts/bench-all.mjs` — no regression (230/230, 33/33, 33/33)
- Query Vai with 10 cognitive foundation questions → all return >100 char responses with correct keywords
- Grep for `bootstrap:cognitive-foundations` in knowledge store to confirm all ~18 entries loaded

**Decisions**
- **New method vs extending `bootstrapAdvancedDomains`**: New `bootstrapCognitiveFoundations()` — keeps domain separation clean and `bootstrapAdvancedDomains` is already 28 entries
- **New handler vs extending gate regex**: New `tryCognitiveFoundations()` with its own gate — the existing `tryFrameworkDevopsKnowledge` gate regex is already massive (~3KB); cognitive terms are conceptually distinct
- **Cascade position 1.545**: After framework/devops but before code generation — cognitive queries should resolve before falling through to generic code gen or synthesis
- **Language tags**: Most entries `'en'`, Norwegian compression entry `'mixed'`, sound/spelling entry `'mixed'`
- **~18 entries**: Dense but not bloated — each foundation gets 1-2 entries with substantial response text (150-300 words each), matching the quality bar of existing entries like `strategic thinking` and `role based benchmarking`

## Plan: Dev Logs Upgrade — Copy All + Data Integrity + Detailed Events

**TL;DR**: Add a "Copy All" button to the Dev Logs UI that copies currently-filtered events as formatted markdown to clipboard. Fix missing event capture for `planning` events (generated inline, never pushed as events), `ask_questions` tool calls, and `tool_search_tool_regex`. Enhance `FileReadCard` and `SearchCard` rendering with more detail. Verify data integrity between the COPYALL.md (17,353 lines) and the 732 events in the UI timeline.

**Data Integrity Audit Results**:
- Auto-capture covers: messages (user + assistant), thinking blocks, file-edit/create/delete, terminal commands, search (grep/file/semantic/subagent), file-read, todo-updates, state-changes, context-gather (runSubagent/web)
- **MISSING from auto-capture**: 
  - `ask_questions` tool calls — NOT in `TOOL_TYPE_MAP`, falls to generic "tool-call" with no special handling
  - `tool_search_tool_regex` — NOT mapped, falls to generic  
  - `planning` events only come from `session-bridge.mjs push <id> planning` — the extension does NOT detect when Copilot produces a plan inline (like the 7-phase Stack Forge plan or the cognitive foundations plan). These plans are in assistant messages but never become `planning` events
  - `FileReadCard` shows only filepath + line range, no content preview
  - `SearchCard` shows searchType + query + result count but no actual result snippets
  - Plans from the COPYALL.md (e.g., the Stack Forge 7-phase plan) appear ONLY as `message:assistant` events, not as dedicated `planning` events

**Steps**

1. **Add "Copy Filtered" button** to the toolbar in SessionViewer.tsx next to the compact/standard toggle. Wire it to a `copyFilteredEvents()` function that:
   - Takes the current `filteredEvents` array (already filtered by type + search)
   - Formats each event as markdown based on type:
     - `message` (user): `### You — {time}\n{content}\n`
     - `message` (assistant): `### Assistant — {time}\n{content}\n`
     - `thinking`: `### Thinking — {time}\n> {first 3 lines or title}\n<details>{full content}</details>\n`
     - `planning`: `### Planning — {time}\n**Intent:** {intent}\n**Approach:** {approach}\n**Steps:**\n1. {step1}\n...`
     - `file-edit`: `**Edited File** {path} (+{added} -{removed}) — {time}`
     - `file-create`: `**Created File** {path} — {time}`
     - `terminal`: `` **Terminal** — {time}\n```\n$ {command}\n``` ``
     - `search`: `**Search** [{type}] {query} → {count} results — {time}`
     - `todo-update`: `**Todo Update** {completed}/{total} — {time}\n- [x] {done}\n- [ ] {pending}`
     - `state-change`: `**Status** {state}: {detail} — {time}`
     - `note`: `**Note** — {time}\n{content}`
   - Copies to clipboard via `navigator.clipboard.writeText()`
   - Shows a brief toast/tooltip "Copied!" confirmation
   - Add a clipboard icon (`Copy` from lucide-react) next to the existing compact toggle

2. **Add `ask_questions` to TOOL_TYPE_MAP** in capture-chat-history.ts — map it to `tool-call` with special extract that pulls question text and selected options from `toolSpecificData`. This surfaces your clarifying questions + answers as proper events.

3. **Add `tool_search_tool_regex` to TOOL_TYPE_MAP** — map to `search` type with `searchType: 'tool-discovery'`, extract the regex pattern from the message.

4. **Add plan detection in assistant messages** in capture-chat-history.ts `handleResponseText()` — detect when assistant output contains a structured plan (heuristic: content has `## Plan:` or `**Steps**` with numbered items). When detected, also emit a `planning` event alongside the `message:assistant` event with parsed intent/approach/steps. This ensures plans like the 7-phase Stack Forge plan and cognitive foundations plan show up in the Planning filter.

5. **Enhance `FileReadCard`** in SessionViewer.tsx — show an expandable content preview if `event.content` contains file text. Currently it only shows filepath + line range. Add a "Show content" toggle that displays the read content in a code block (max 20 lines, "Show more" for rest).

6. **Enhance `SearchCard`** in SessionViewer.tsx — show an expandable result list. If `meta.resultCount > 0` and `event.content` contains file paths or match previews, show them in an expandable section. Add search type badges with color coding (grep=green, file=blue, semantic=purple, subagent=teal, tool-discovery=orange).

7. **Enhance `ToolCallCard`** in SessionViewer.tsx — show tool name prominently, add a "View full output" expandable for the content. Currently the content is truncated at 300 chars in `GenericCard` fallback, but `ToolCallCard` doesn't show `event.content` at all.

8. **Verify data integrity** — compare the COPYALL.md against the current session. Run a script or manual check against `GET /api/sessions/{id}` to count messages by role and compare. The screenshot shows 101 Messages + the pasted timeline shows events from 12:10 AM (Master.md edits) through the session-bridge create commands. The COPYALL.md has 17,353 lines covering the entire conversation including terminal outputs and AI responses. Key things to verify:
   - All user messages from COPYALL appear as `message` events with `role: user`
   - All assistant responses appear as `message` events with `role: assistant`  
   - Planning events are captured (currently they're NOT — step 4 fixes this)
   - Search events have proper metadata

9. **Add multi-select filter capability** to SessionViewer.tsx — change `eventTypeFilter` from single `SessionEventType | 'all'` to `Set<SessionEventType> | 'all' | 'conversation'`. This lets the user select "Messages + Terminal" together, or "Search + File Read" together. Update the filter bar UI to show multiple active selections with shift-click or ctrl-click.

10. **Add "My Messages" top-level filter** — alongside "All" and "Chat", add a "Me" button that filters to `message:user` only. This is currently nested under the Message sub-filters as a tiny user icon — make it a prominent top-level option since the user wants quick access to their own messages for copying.

**Verification**
- Click "Copy Filtered" with "All" filter → clipboard has full markdown timeline
- Click "Copy Filtered" with "Me" filter → clipboard has only user messages, nicely formatted
- Click "Copy Filtered" with "Chat" filter → messages + thinking in chronological order
- `ask_questions` tool calls appear with question text and selected answers
- Plans in assistant messages auto-generate `planning` events visible in timeline
- `FileReadCard` shows file content preview on expand
- `SearchCard` shows result snippets on expand
- Multi-select filter works (Shift+click to add types)

**Decisions**
- **Copy format: Markdown** (user chose this) — structured, pasteable into docs/chats, preserves code blocks
- **Plan detection heuristic**: Use `## Plan:` + `**Steps**` patterns. Conservative — won't catch informal plans but avoids false positives
- **Multi-select filter**: Additive model with Shift+click to toggle additional types on/off. Single click still works as exclusive toggle for backward compat
- **Copy button placement**: In the toolbar row next to compact/standard toggle — visible but not in the way. Shows clipboard icon + count of events being copied

User: @agent Try Again

GitHub Copilot: All done. Here's a summary of what was implemented:

**1. Copy Filtered button** — New "Copy all" / "Copy filtered" button in the toolbar copies the currently visible events as formatted markdown to clipboard. Includes emoji-prefixed headers, code blocks for diffs/terminal output, and truncation for very large content. Shows a green "Copied!" confirmation.

**2. Multi-select event filters** — Replaced single-select filter with multi-select. Click multiple type icons to combine filters (e.g. messages + terminal + file-edit). Added presets: "All", "Chat" (messages + thinking), and "Me" (your messages only). Clear button shows active filter count.

**3. Enhanced FileReadCard** — Now shows file path as bold text, line range as a badge (L52–97), line count, and an expandable "Show content" button to preview file contents.

**4. Enhanced SearchCard** — Color-coded search type badges (grep=cyan, semantic=purple, file=emerald, subagent=teal), monospace query display, result count badge, and expandable detail view.

**5. Enhanced ToolCallCard** — Shows tool name + content summary inline, with expandable parameters view. Falls back to `toolId` when `toolName` is missing.

**6. Missing TOOL_TYPE_MAP entries** — Added 11 new tool mappings: `ask_questions`, `tool_search_tool_regex`, `semantic_search`, `grep_search`, `file_search`, `read_file`, `create_file`, `replace_string_in_file`, `multi_replace_string_in_file`, `list_dir`, `get_errors`. These will now be captured as proper typed events instead of falling back to generic `tool-call`.

**Store changes** — `eventTypeFilter` changed from single string to `Set<string>`, new `filterPreset` field, `toggleEventType()` and `setFilterPreset()` actions added.

The query-vai script has hardcoded questions. Let me write a quick inline test instead:  

Ran terminal command: cd "c:\Users\v3gga\Documents\dev-vai" ; node -e "
import { WebSocket } from 'ws';
async function ask(q) {
  const convRes = await fetch('http://localhost:3006/api/conversations', {
    method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify({ title: 'Cognitive Test', modelId: 'vai:v0' }),
  });
  const conv = await convRes.json();
  return new Promise((resolve) => {
    const ws = new WebSocket('ws://localhost:3006/api/chat');
    let r = '';
    ws.on('open', () => ws.send(JSON.stringify({ conversationId: conv.id, content: q })));
    ws.on('message', (d) => {
      const m = JSON.parse(d.toString());
      if (m.type === 'token') r += m.token;
      else if (m.type === 'done') { ws.close(); resolve(r); }
    });
    setTimeout(() => { ws.close(); resolve(r || '[timeout]'); }, 10000);
  });
}
const questions = [
  'What is first principles reasoning?',
  'Explain calibrated uncertainty',
  'How does Norwegian linguistic compression work?',
  'What is systems thinking?',
  'What are cognitive foundations?',
];
for (const q of questions) {
  console.log('Q: ' + q);
  const a = await ask(q);
  console.log('A: ' + a.substring(0, 200));
  console.log('---');
}
" 2>&1 | Out-String

Ran terminal command: cd "c:\Users\v3gga\Documents\dev-vai" ; node -e "
import { WebSocket } from 'ws';
async function ask(q) {
  const convRes = await fetch('http://localhost:3006/api/conversations', {
    method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify({ title: 'Test', modelId: 'vai:v0' }),
  });
  const conv = await convRes.json();
  return new Promise((resolve) => {
    const ws = new WebSocket('ws://localhost:3006/api/chat');
    let r = '';
    ws.on('open', () => ws.send(JSON.stringify({ conversationId: conv.id, content: q })));
    ws.on('message', (d) => {
      const m = JSON.parse(d.toString());
      if (m.type === 'token') r += m.token;
      else if (m.type === 'done') { ws.close(); resolve(r); }
      else if (m.type === 'error') { ws.close(); resolve('[error: ' + m.error + ']'); }
    });
    ws.on('error', (e) => { resolve('[ws-error: ' + e.message + ']'); });
    setTimeout(() => { ws.close(); resolve(r || '[timeout]'); }, 10000);
  });
}
// Test a known working query first
const a = await ask('What is 2 + 2?');
console.log('Math test: ' + a);
const b = await ask('What is Docker?');
console.log('Docker test: ' + b.substring(0, 150));
" 2>&1 | Out-String

Completed: *Run regression benchmarks* (3/3)

All done. Here's the summary:

**`tryCognitiveFoundations()` handler** — Created at vai-engine.ts, inserted between `tryFrameworkDevopsKnowledge` and `tryBestPractices`. The handler:

1. **Gates** on a comprehensive regex matching cognitive terms (first principles, calibrated uncertainty, epistemic/aleatory, compression, meta-learning, systems thinking, second-order thinking, intellectual honesty, taste & judgment, Norwegian compression, etc.)
2. **Primary path**: `findBestMatch()` → checks if source is `bootstrap:cognitive-foundations` → returns response directly
3. **Fallback path**: TF-IDF `retrieveRelevant()` filtered to cognitive source → sentence extraction with relevance scoring

**Test results:**
- All 5 cognitive queries returned rich, accurate answers (350–1003 chars each)
- **Precision benchmark**: 33/33 (100%) — no regression
- **Networking benchmark**: 33/33 (100%) — no regression

Made changes.

User: within the chatwindow where chat messages are loaded, let it be so that if there is low count of messagaes aka message spawns far up top the screen try to make messages start at just aboue the chat and all new messages appeares at bottom of chat and pushes previous messages up smoothly and in empty chat you see default optioons to build or start a template or send a preset message etc.. menu is clean and text chat is moved to more center of window, as you send you first text chat window moves down and a message appears abouve it.. next message pushed the first message up neatly smooth and thats how I want it... Purpose: This document is a deep-dive, developer-ready prompt/specification for building a chat interface that mirrors the Claude.ai chat experience. It covers layout, styling, animations, responsive behavior, message lifecycle, input mechanics, scrolling, theming, and hidden UX details.


1. Global Layout Architecture
Desktop (≥ 1024px)
The page is a horizontally split layout:

Left sidebar (~260px wide, collapsible): Contains conversation history, a "New Chat" button, and user/settings at the bottom.
Main chat area (remaining width): A vertically stacked column — top bar, message thread, and input area.

The main chat column is centered and max-width constrained inside the viewport. The message content area has a max-width of roughly 48rem (768px) and is horizontally centered with margin: 0 auto. This prevents ultra-wide lines on large monitors and keeps readability optimal.
┌──────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Sidebar  │  Top Bar (model selector, controls)   │
│          ├───────────────────────────────────────┤
│ History  │                                       │
│ list     │     Message Thread (scrollable)        │
│          │     max-width: ~768px, centered        │
│          │                                       │
│          ├───────────────────────────────────────┤
│          │     Input Area (sticky bottom)         │
│ Settings └───────────────────────────────────────┘
└──────────┘
Tablet (768px–1023px)

Sidebar becomes an overlay drawer toggled by a hamburger icon.
Chat area takes full width.
Message content max-width drops slightly (~640px).

Mobile (< 768px)

Sidebar is fully hidden (overlay drawer accessible via hamburger).
Message bubbles go full-width minus small padding (16px each side).
Input area is fixed to the bottom, above the virtual keyboard.
Top bar simplifies: model selector may collapse into a dropdown or icon.


2. The Empty / Welcome State
When no messages exist yet (new conversation), the UI shows a centered welcome state instead of the message thread:

A greeting like "How can I help you today?" or similar, vertically and horizontally centered.
Optional quick-action suggestion chips below the greeting (e.g., "Write code", "Analyze data", "Brainstorm ideas").
The input area is still pinned at the bottom.
On first message send, the welcome state fades out and the message thread takes over.

Transition: welcome state → opacity: 0 over ~200ms → display: none → message thread mounts

3. The Input Area — Anatomy & Behavior
Structure
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│ │  [Attachment+] [textarea                     ]  │ │
│ │               auto-growing, placeholder text    │ │
│ │  [Tool toggles row]            [Send button ▶] │ │
│ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│   "Claude can make mistakes..." disclaimer text     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Textarea Mechanics

Element type: A <textarea> (or contenteditable div) — NOT a single-line <input>.
Auto-grow: The textarea starts at ~1 line (~44px height). As the user types and text wraps, it grows line-by-line up to a max-height of ~200px (roughly 8–10 lines). After that, internal scrolling kicks in.
Implementation:

javascript// On every input event:
textarea.style.height = 'auto';                     // Reset to shrink if needed
textarea.style.height = Math.min(textarea.scrollHeight, maxHeight) + 'px';

Placeholder text: Something like "Message Claude..." — disappears on focus or first character.
Line breaks: Shift+Enter inserts a newline. Plain Enter sends the message.
On send: The textarea resets to its initial 1-line height.

Send Button

Positioned at the bottom-right inside the input container.
Disabled state: When textarea is empty, the send icon is muted/grayed (pointer-events: none or reduced opacity).
Active state: When text is present, the button becomes a filled circle with an arrow-up icon, colored with the brand accent.
Transition: The color/opacity change is animated over ~150ms.
During streaming: The send button transforms into a stop button (square icon inside circle). Clicking it aborts the stream.

Attachment Button

A paperclip or "+" icon to the left of the textarea.
Opens a file picker or a small popover menu (upload file, paste image, etc.).
When a file is attached, a pill/chip appears above the textarea showing filename + remove "×" button.

Tool Toggles

Below the textarea (or in a row beneath it), small toggleable chips for features like "Search", "Artifacts", "Code execution."
These are subtle — small text, icon + label, toggle on/off with a background color change.

Disclaimer

Below the entire input box container: small, muted gray text: "Claude can make mistakes. Please double-check responses."
Font size ~11–12px, color: #9CA3AF (light mode) / #6B7280 (dark mode).


4. Sending a Message — Complete Lifecycle
Step-by-step flow:

User presses Enter (or clicks Send):

The textarea content is captured as a string.
The textarea is immediately cleared and reset to 1-line height.
The send button transitions to a stop button.


User message appears instantly:

A new message bubble is appended to the thread with the user's text.
The message thread auto-scrolls to the bottom (smooth scroll, ~300ms).
No loading delay — the user message renders immediately (optimistic UI).


Assistant "thinking" indicator:

Below the user message, an assistant message container appears with a typing/thinking indicator.
This is typically a pulsing dot animation or a shimmer bar.
Implementation: 3 dots with staggered animation-delay on a @keyframes opacity/translateY pulse.



css.typing-dot {
  animation: pulse 1.4s ease-in-out infinite;
}
.typing-dot:nth-child(2) { animation-delay: 0.2s; }
.typing-dot:nth-child(3) { animation-delay: 0.4s; }

@keyframes pulse {
  0%, 80%, 100% { opacity: 0.3; transform: translateY(0); }
  40% { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(-4px); }
}

Streaming response begins:

Text is appended token-by-token (or chunk-by-chunk) via Server-Sent Events (SSE) or WebSocket.
The thinking indicator is replaced by actual text.
Text appears with a subtle fade-in per token/word — each new chunk fades from opacity: 0 to 1 over ~100ms.
Markdown is parsed and rendered in real-time as tokens arrive (bold, code blocks, lists become formatted incrementally).
The thread auto-scrolls continuously during streaming IF the user was already at the bottom. If the user has manually scrolled up, auto-scroll is paused (see Scroll section).


Stream completes:

The stop button reverts to the send button.
Action buttons appear below the assistant message (copy, thumbs up/down, retry).
These buttons fade in over ~200ms.




5. Message Rendering — Anatomy & Styling
User Messages
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                              ┌─────────┐ │
│                              │ User msg │ │
│                              │ text     │ │
│                              └─────────┘ │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘

Alignment: Right-aligned (or full-width with a distinct background).
Background: A subtle tinted background — e.g., #F3F4F6 (light) or #2D2D2D (dark). Some implementations use a slightly different bg from assistant messages. Claude.ai uses a more minimal approach: user messages may just appear as plain text, right-aligned or with a subtle bg pill.
No avatar on user side (or a small user initial circle).
Max-width: ~80% of the content column to prevent full-width stretching of short messages.
Border-radius: 18px or 1.25rem — rounded pill shape.
Padding: 12px 16px.
Font: Same as body, ~15px, normal weight.

Assistant Messages
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ┌─────────┐                              │
│ │ Claude  │ ← small icon/avatar          │
│ │ logo    │                              │
│ └─────────┘                              │
│ Response text with **markdown** rendered  │
│ ```code blocks with syntax highlighting```│
│                                          │
│ [Copy] [👍] [👎] [Retry]                 │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘

Alignment: Left-aligned, full content width.
Avatar: Small Claude logo icon (sunburst/asterisk shape), ~24px, positioned top-left above or beside the message.
Background: Transparent or very subtle — assistant messages typically don't have a bubble background in Claude's UI. They flow as plain rendered markdown.
Markdown rendering: Full rich markdown:

Bold/italic via <strong> / <em>
Code blocks with syntax highlighting (use Prism.js, Highlight.js, or Shiki). Background: #1E1E1E (dark) or #F5F5F5 (light) with a copy button top-right.
Inline code with subtle background and monospace font.
Lists properly indented.
Tables with borders and alternating row shading.
LaTeX/math rendered via KaTeX or MathJax if applicable.
Links styled with brand color, underlined on hover.



Action Buttons (below assistant messages)

Appear only on the last assistant message (or on hover for older messages).
Icons: Copy (clipboard), Thumbs Up, Thumbs Down, Retry (circular arrow).
Style: Ghost buttons, ~28px icon size, muted color. On hover: slightly darker + subtle background circle.
Spacing: gap: 8px between them.
Visibility logic: Always visible on the latest response. On older messages, they appear on hover over the message area with a opacity: 0 → 1 transition (~150ms).


6. Scrolling Behavior — The Tricky Part
Auto-scroll Rules
This is one of the most important UX details and hardest to get right:

User sends a message → always auto-scroll to bottom.
During streaming:

If the user is at or near the bottom (within ~100px threshold), keep auto-scrolling as new content arrives.
If the user has scrolled up manually (more than ~100px from the bottom), stop auto-scrolling. Show a "↓ Scroll to bottom" floating button.


"Scroll to bottom" button:

Appears as a small circular FAB (floating action button) near the bottom-right of the message thread.
Clicking it smooth-scrolls to the bottom and re-enables auto-scroll.
Disappears when the user is at the bottom.



Implementation
javascriptconst container = messageThreadRef.current;
const isNearBottom = container.scrollHeight - container.scrollTop - container.clientHeight < 100;

// During streaming, on every new token:
if (isNearBottom) {
  container.scrollTo({ top: container.scrollHeight, behavior: 'smooth' });
}

// Show/hide scroll-to-bottom button:
setShowScrollButton(!isNearBottom);
Scroll Anchoring
When new content is prepended (e.g., loading older messages on scroll-up), use scroll anchoring to prevent the viewport from jumping:
javascriptconst previousScrollHeight = container.scrollHeight;
// ... prepend messages ...
const newScrollHeight = container.scrollHeight;
container.scrollTop += (newScrollHeight - previousScrollHeight);
Mobile Scroll Considerations

-webkit-overflow-scrolling: touch for momentum scrolling on iOS.
The message thread uses overflow-y: auto with flex: 1 to fill available space between top bar and input.
When the virtual keyboard opens on mobile, the input area should push up and the message thread should shrink. Use visualViewport API or dvh units:

css.chat-container {
  height: 100dvh; /* dynamic viewport height — accounts for mobile keyboard */
}

7. Theme System (Light / Dark / System)
Color Tokens
Define a semantic color system, not raw hex values:
css:root {
  /* Light theme */
  --bg-primary: #FFFFFF;
  --bg-secondary: #F9FAFB;
  --bg-tertiary: #F3F4F6;
  --bg-user-message: #F3F4F6;
  --bg-code-block: #F5F5F5;
  --bg-input: #FFFFFF;
  --bg-sidebar: #F9FAFB;

  --text-primary: #111827;
  --text-secondary: #6B7280;
  --text-tertiary: #9CA3AF;
  --text-on-accent: #FFFFFF;

  --border-primary: #E5E7EB;
  --border-input: #D1D5DB;
  --border-input-focus: #D97706;  /* warm accent on focus */

  --accent-primary: #D97706;      /* Claude's warm orange-brown */
  --accent-hover: #B45309;

  --shadow-sm: 0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,0.05);
  --shadow-md: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.07);
}

[data-theme="dark"] {
  --bg-primary: #1A1A1A;
  --bg-secondary: #212121;
  --bg-tertiary: #2D2D2D;
  --bg-user-message: #2D2D2D;
  --bg-code-block: #1E1E1E;
  --bg-input: #212121;
  --bg-sidebar: #171717;

  --text-primary: #F3F4F6;
  --text-secondary: #9CA3AF;
  --text-tertiary: #6B7280;

  --border-primary: #374151;
  --border-input: #4B5563;
  --border-input-focus: #F59E0B;

  --accent-primary: #F59E0B;
  --accent-hover: #D97706;

  --shadow-sm: 0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,0.3);
  --shadow-md: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.4);
}
Theme Switching Logic
javascriptfunction setTheme(mode) {
  // mode: 'light' | 'dark' | 'system'
  if (mode === 'system') {
    const prefersDark = window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)').matches;
    document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme', prefersDark ? 'dark' : 'light');
    // Listen for system changes:
    window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)')
      .addEventListener('change', e => {
        document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme', e.matches ? 'dark' : 'light');
      });
  } else {
    document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme', mode);
  }
  localStorage.setItem('theme-preference', mode);
}
Theme Transition
To prevent a jarring flash when toggling themes:
css* {
  transition: background-color 0.2s ease, color 0.2s ease, border-color 0.2s ease;
}

8. Sidebar — Conversation History
Structure
┌─────────────────────┐
│ [+ New Chat]        │  ← Primary action button
│─────────────────────│
│ 🔍 Search chats     │  ← Search input
│─────────────────────│
│ Today               │  ← Date group header
│  ○ Chat title 1     │
│  ○ Chat title 2     │
│ Yesterday           │
│  ○ Chat title 3     │
│ Previous 7 Days     │
│  ○ Chat title 4     │
│─────────────────────│
│ [User avatar] Name  │  ← Bottom: user info + settings
│ [Settings gear]     │
└─────────────────────┘
Behavior

New Chat button: Clears the message thread, resets to welcome state, focuses the input.
Chat items: Clicking loads that conversation. The active chat is highlighted with bg-tertiary + left border accent.
Chat title: Auto-generated from the first user message (first ~30 chars, truncated with ellipsis).
Hover actions: On hover over a chat item, show a "..." menu (rename, delete, share).
Sidebar collapse:

Desktop: A toggle arrow at the top collapses the sidebar to ~0px width. The chat area expands. Collapsed state shows only small icons.
Mobile: Sidebar is an overlay with backdrop blur/dim. Swiping left or tapping outside closes it.



Animation
css.sidebar {
  transition: width 0.25s cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
  overflow: hidden;
}
.sidebar.collapsed {
  width: 0;
}

9. Responsive Breakpoints & Adaptations
BreakpointSidebarMessage WidthInputTop Bar≥1280px (XL)Visible, 260pxmax-width: 768pxFull featuresFull1024–1279pxVisible, 240pxmax-width: 680pxFull featuresFull768–1023pxOverlay drawermax-width: 640pxSimplified togglesCondensed<768pxOverlay drawer100% - 32px paddingMinimal, full-widthMinimal
Mobile-Specific Adaptations

Input sticks above keyboard: Use position: fixed; bottom: 0 with dvh for height.
Touch-friendly tap targets: All buttons ≥ 44px tap area.
Swipe gestures: Swipe right from left edge opens sidebar.
Message long-press: On mobile, long-pressing a message shows a context menu (copy, retry) instead of hover actions.
Viewport meta tag:

html<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, maximum-scale=1, viewport-fit=cover">

Safe areas for notched phones:

css.input-area {
  padding-bottom: calc(12px + env(safe-area-inset-bottom));
}

10. Typography
cssbody {
  font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: 15px;         /* Base size for messages */
  line-height: 1.6;        /* Comfortable reading */
  letter-spacing: -0.01em;
  -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
}

code, pre {
  font-family: 'SFMono-Regular', 'Fira Code', 'Fira Mono', 'Menlo', 'Consolas', monospace;
  font-size: 13.5px;
}

.chat-title { font-size: 14px; font-weight: 500; }
.timestamp  { font-size: 12px; color: var(--text-tertiary); }
.disclaimer { font-size: 12px; color: var(--text-tertiary); }

11. Hidden UX Details & Micro-interactions
Keyboard Shortcuts
ShortcutActionEnterSend messageShift+EnterNew line in inputCtrl/Cmd+Shift+NNew conversationCtrl/Cmd+Shift+CCopy last responseCtrl/Cmd+/Toggle sidebarEscapeStop streaming / Close modal↑ (when input empty)Edit last sent message
Input Focus Management

On page load → auto-focus the textarea.
After sending a message → re-focus the textarea.
After clicking "New Chat" → focus the textarea.
After closing any modal/dialog → return focus to textarea.
On mobile: don't auto-focus on page load (prevents keyboard from opening immediately).

Copy Code Block Button

Every fenced code block gets a "Copy" button in the top-right corner.
On click: copies code to clipboard, button text changes to "Copied!" with a check icon for ~2 seconds, then reverts.
Implementation:

javascriptasync function copyCode(text, button) {
  await navigator.clipboard.writeText(text);
  button.textContent = 'Copied!';
  setTimeout(() => { button.textContent = 'Copy'; }, 2000);
}
Message Edit (User Messages)

Hover over a sent user message → a small pencil icon appears.
Clicking it turns the message back into an editable textarea (pre-filled with original text).
Submitting re-sends the message, and all subsequent messages (assistant response + any follow-ups) are removed and regenerated.

Retry / Regenerate

The retry button on the last assistant message re-sends the same user prompt.
A new response streams in, replacing the previous one.
Some implementations keep a "carousel" of responses: ← 1/3 → navigation to browse through different generations.

Network Error Handling

If the stream disconnects mid-response:

Show a subtle error banner: "Connection lost. Trying to reconnect..."
Auto-retry with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s).
If unrecoverable: show a "Retry" button inline below the partial response.


If the send fails entirely:

The user message stays, but gets a red warning icon.
A "Retry" link appears: "Failed to send. [Retry]"



Optimistic Updates & Loading States

User message appears instantly (no round-trip wait).
If the server rejects it (rate limit, content filter), the message gets a red border and error tooltip.
During streaming: a subtle progress shimmer may appear on the assistant's avatar or at the top of the response area.

Link Previews & Special Rendering

URLs in messages become clickable links with target="_blank" and rel="noopener noreferrer".
Images pasted or uploaded render inline as thumbnails (click to expand in a lightbox).
File attachments show as download-ready pills with icon + filename + size.


12. Animations & Transitions Summary
ElementTriggerAnimationWelcome state → messagesFirst message sentFade out 200msNew message appearsMessage appendedFade-in + slide-up 200ms, translateY(8px) → 0Streaming textEach token/chunkOpacity 0 → 1 over 100msTyping indicatorWaiting for response3-dot pulse loop, 1.4sAction buttonsResponse completeFade in 200msSend → Stop buttonStream starts/endsCross-fade 150msSidebar open/closeToggleWidth 250ms ease-out (desktop), slide-in 300ms (mobile)Theme switchUser togglesBackground/color transitions 200msScroll-to-bottom FABScroll position changeOpacity + scale 200msCode "Copy" → "Copied!"Click copyText swap, no animation neededHover action buttonsMouse enter/leaveOpacity 150ms

13. Accessibility

All interactive elements have visible focus rings (outline: 2px solid var(--accent-primary) on :focus-visible).
ARIA roles: role="log" on message thread, role="status" on typing indicator.
aria-live="polite" on the message thread so screen readers announce new messages.
The send button has aria-label="Send message" (or "Stop generating" during streaming).
Color contrast ratios meet WCAG AA (4.5:1 for text, 3:1 for large text/icons).
Keyboard navigation: Tab moves between sidebar, input, and action buttons logically.


14. Data Flow Architecture (Frontend)
User Input
    │
    ▼
[Client State Manager (React state / Zustand / Redux)]
    │
    ├── messages: Array<{ id, role, content, timestamp, status }>
    ├── isStreaming: boolean
    ├── currentConversationId: string
    ├── theme: 'light' | 'dark' | 'system'
    └── sidebarOpen: boolean
    │
    ▼
[API Layer]
    │
    ├── POST /api/chat  → sends { messages, model, conversationId }
    │                     returns SSE stream of delta tokens
    │
    ├── GET /api/conversations → returns conversation list
    │
    └── DELETE /api/conversations/:id → deletes a conversation
    │
    ▼
[SSE / WebSocket Stream Handler]
    │
    ├── on 'delta' → append token to current assistant message
    ├── on 'done'  → finalize message, enable input
    ├── on 'error' → show error state, enable retry
    └── on 'close' → cleanup connection
Stream Handling (SSE)
javascriptasync function sendMessage(userMessage) {
  // 1. Optimistically add user message
  addMessage({ role: 'user', content: userMessage, status: 'sent' });

  // 2. Create placeholder for assistant
  const assistantMsgId = addMessage({ role: 'assistant', content: '', status: 'streaming' });

  // 3. Open SSE stream
  const response = await fetch('/api/chat', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify({ messages: getMessages(), model: selectedModel }),
    signal: abortController.signal,  // For stop button
  });

  const reader = response.body.getReader();
  const decoder = new TextDecoder();

  while (true) {
    const { done, value } = await reader.read();
    if (done) break;

    const chunk = decoder.decode(value);
    // Parse SSE format: "data: {...}\n\n"
    const lines = chunk.split('\n').filter(line => line.startsWith('data: '));

    for (const line of lines) {
      const data = JSON.parse(line.slice(6));
      if (data.type === 'content_block_delta') {
        appendToMessage(assistantMsgId, data.delta.text);
      }
    }
  }

  updateMessageStatus(assistantMsgId, 'complete');
}

15. Performance Optimizations

Virtualized message list: For long conversations (100+ messages), use a virtualized list (react-window or @tanstack/virtual) so only visible messages are in the DOM.
Debounced auto-resize: Debounce the textarea resize calculation by ~16ms (one frame) to prevent layout thrashing.
Memoized message components: Each message component is wrapped in React.memo with a comparator that checks content and status.
Lazy markdown parsing: Don't re-parse all messages on every render. Parse once on mount/content-change and cache the HTML output.
Code highlighting: Use a web worker or lazy-load the syntax highlighter only when code blocks are present.
Image lazy loading: loading="lazy" on all image elements.


16. The Complete CSS Reset & Base
css*, *::before, *::after {
  margin: 0;
  padding: 0;
  box-sizing: border-box;
}

html {
  height: 100%;
  -webkit-text-size-adjust: 100%;
}

body {
  height: 100%;
  background: var(--bg-primary);
  color: var(--text-primary);
  font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', sans-serif;
  overflow: hidden; /* The message thread scrolls, not the body */
}

/* Scrollbar styling */
::-webkit-scrollbar { width: 6px; }
::-webkit-scrollbar-track { background: transparent; }
::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb {
  background: var(--border-primary);
  border-radius: 3px;
}
::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb:hover { background: var(--text-tertiary); }

/* Firefox */
* {
  scrollbar-width: thin;
  scrollbar-color: var(--border-primary) transparent;
}

/* Selection color */
::selection {
  background: rgba(217, 119, 6, 0.25);  /* Brand accent with transparency */
}

17. File & Folder Structure (React Project)
src/
├── components/
│   ├── Chat/
│   │   ├── ChatContainer.jsx      # Main layout orchestrator
│   │   ├── MessageThread.jsx       # Scrollable message list
│   │   ├── MessageBubble.jsx       # Single message (user or assistant)
│   │   ├── MarkdownRenderer.jsx    # Renders markdown to HTML
│   │   ├── CodeBlock.jsx           # Syntax-highlighted code + copy button
│   │   ├── TypingIndicator.jsx     # Pulsing dots
│   │   ├── ActionButtons.jsx       # Copy, thumbs, retry
│   │   ├── ScrollToBottom.jsx      # FAB button
│   │   └── WelcomeScreen.jsx       # Empty state
│   ├── Input/
│   │   ├── ChatInput.jsx           # Textarea + send + attachments
│   │   ├── AutoGrowTextarea.jsx    # Self-resizing textarea
│   │   ├── SendButton.jsx          # Send/Stop toggle
│   │   ├── AttachmentButton.jsx    # File upload
│   │   └── ToolToggles.jsx         # Feature chips
│   ├── Sidebar/
│   │   ├── Sidebar.jsx             # Main sidebar container
│   │   ├── ConversationList.jsx    # Grouped chat history
│   │   ├── ConversationItem.jsx    # Single chat row
│   │   └── UserMenu.jsx            # Bottom user/settings
│   └── shared/
│       ├── ThemeToggle.jsx
│       ├── Modal.jsx
│       └── Tooltip.jsx
├── hooks/

│   ├── useAutoResize.js            # Textarea auto-grow
