└── App.jsx

Summary Checklist

 Centered, max-width message column
 Auto-growing textarea with Enter to send, Shift+Enter for newline
 Optimistic user message rendering
 SSE streaming with token-by-token display
 Typing indicator with pulsing dots
 Smart auto-scroll with manual-scroll detection
 Scroll-to-bottom floating button
 Dark / Light / System theme with CSS variables
 Collapsible sidebar with conversation history
 Responsive: sidebar drawer on mobile, full-width messages
 100dvh layout for mobile keyboard handling
 Code blocks with syntax highlighting and copy button
 Message action buttons (copy, rate, retry) on hover / last message
 Send ↔ Stop button toggle during streaming
 Keyboard shortcuts
 Accessibility (ARIA, focus management, contrast)
 Virtualized list for long conversations
 Error handling with retry UI
 Safe area insets for notched devices
 Smooth transitions on all state changes' and or 'VS Code Chat & Agent Sessions — Complete Deep Dive

Purpose: A comprehensive breakdown of how VS Code's Chat and Agent Sessions system works — the UI surfaces, session lifecycle, agent orchestration, context management, checkpoints, message handling, and all the hidden mechanics that make it tick.


1. What Is a Chat Session?
A chat session in VS Code is a single, isolated conversation between you and an AI model. It includes every prompt you've sent, every response you've received, and all the context (files, symbols, terminal output) that was referenced during that conversation. Each session maintains its own context window — a running accumulation of everything the AI "remembers" within that conversation. When you start a new session, that context is wiped clean.
Key properties of a session:

Independent context: Session A knows nothing about Session B.
Persistent history: Even after starting a new session, old sessions are saved and can be reopened.
Movable across views: A session can live in the sidebar, an editor tab, or a separate window — and you can move it between these at any time without losing state.
Multi-agent aware: A single session can involve local agents, background agents, cloud agents, and third-party agents.


2. The Three Chat Surfaces
VS Code doesn't confine chat to one location. There are three distinct surfaces where a session can live, each suited to a different workflow.
Sidebar Chat (Default)

Lives in the left or right sidebar alongside the file explorer, source control, etc.
Best for keeping chat visible while you code.
Narrow by default (~300–400px). When you make the sidebar wider, it automatically switches to a side-by-side mode where the chat and your code share the panel.
Shortcut: Ctrl+N / ⌘N opens a new session here by default.

Editor Tab Chat

Opens the chat as a full editor tab, just like a code file.
Best for giving chat more space, comparing sessions side by side (split tabs), or when you want chat to behave like a document.
Shortcut: New Chat (+) → New Chat Editor or the command Chat: New Chat Editor.

Separate Window Chat

Opens chat in an entirely separate OS window.
Best for multi-monitor setups — chat on one screen, code on the other.
Shortcut: New Chat (+) → New Chat Window.

You can move an existing session between any of these surfaces at any time via the ... menu on the chat or the Command Palette (Chat: Move Chat into Editor Area, Chat: Move Chat into Side Bar, Chat: Move Chat into New Window). The full conversation history and context are preserved during the move.

3. Anatomy of the Chat UI
The Input Area
The chat input box is a multi-line text field at the bottom of the chat surface. It's more than a simple textarea — it's context-aware and interactive.
Components inside/around the input:

Agent selector dropdown (top-left of input or above it): Lets you switch between built-in agents (Agent, Plan, Ask) or any custom agents you've created. This determines the AI's persona and capabilities for the session.
Model picker (near the agent selector): Choose which language model powers the session (GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet, Gemini, etc.). You can customize which models appear here.
Context indicator: Shows a visual representation of how much of the context window has been consumed. This fills up as the conversation grows. When it's nearly full, starting a new session is recommended.
# mentions: Typing # in the input opens a picker for explicitly attaching context — files (#file), folders, symbols, your entire codebase (#codebase), terminal output (#terminalSelection), tools like #fetch, #githubRepo, and special tools like #runSubagent.
@ mentions: Typing @ invokes specialized chat participants like @vscode, @terminal, or @workspace.
Attached context chips: When you reference files or context, they appear as removable chips/pills above or within the input. Press Delete to remove them.

Send Button Behavior
The send button is not just a simple "send." During an active response, it transforms into a dropdown with three options (experimental feature called message steering and queuing):

Add to Queue: Your message waits and sends automatically after the current response finishes.
Steer with Message: Signals the current request to yield after its current tool execution, then processes your new message immediately — essentially course-correcting the agent mid-run.
Stop and Send: Cancels the current request entirely and sends your new message.

When you have multiple pending messages (queued or steering), you can drag and drop them to reorder the queue. A drag handle appears on hover when multiple messages are pending.
Message Rendering

User messages: Appear as plain text blocks, right-aligned or in a distinct style. They support inline editing — click the edit icon (pencil) on any previous user message to revise and resend it. This is now the default behavior (inline editing), not a separate mode.
Assistant messages: Rendered with full markdown — code blocks with syntax highlighting, tables, math (KaTeX via chat.math.enabled), links, and structured output. Tool calls and their results are shown as collapsible sections with summarized titles.
Chain-of-thought display: When the model reasons internally, those reasoning steps can be shown as collapsible sections within the response.
Task list tracking (experimental): The AI can generate task breakdowns that show real-time progress within the chat — checkboxes that update as the agent completes steps.


4. The Agent Sessions View ("Agent HQ")
This is the centerpiece of VS Code's multi-agent orchestration. It's a dedicated sidebar view called "Agent Sessions" that acts as mission control for all your AI agents.
What It Shows

All active and past sessions across every agent type: local Copilot, Copilot coding agent (cloud), GitHub Copilot CLI, OpenAI Codex, and any third-party agents.
Session status: Running, completed, waiting for input, errored.
Rich descriptions: Each session shows contextual details about what it's doing.
Progress info: For running sessions, it shows the last tool call's progress instead of just a generic "working..." message.
Changed files and associated PRs: For cloud agents, you can see which files were modified and any pull requests created.
Grouping: Sessions are grouped by state and age when displayed side by side.

Session Management Actions

"+" button in the view header to quickly start a new session.
Context menu (right-click): Archive, mark as read/unread, open, delete.
Keyboard access: Archive, change read state, and open sessions without touching the mouse.
Batch operations: Archive multiple sessions at once from group sections.
Status bar tracking: Monitor multiple running agents from the VS Code status bar.

Unified Single-View Mode
In the latest iterations, the Agent Sessions view supports a unified single-view mode that displays all sessions from different providers in one place, with filtering, searching, and session-state management at a glance. No more switching between separate views for Copilot vs. Codex vs. CLI.

5. Session Types — Where the AI Runs
When you create a session, you're choosing not just what agent to use, but where it runs. VS Code supports four session environments:
Local Sessions

The AI runs within your VS Code instance on your machine.
Has direct access to your workspace files, terminal, and extensions.
Best for interactive, real-time coding assistance.

Background Sessions (formerly CLI Sessions)

Runs in a separate process using the GitHub Copilot CLI.
Can run in an isolated Git worktree — VS Code spins up a dedicated worktree per session so file changes live in a separate folder. Multiple background agents can work simultaneously without conflicting edits.
You can review and merge worktree changes back into the main workspace, or apply them directly when the agent completes.
Accepts rich attached context: selections, problems, symbols, search results, and more.
Terminal output is rendered using an embedded xterm.js terminal instance with full ANSI color support (not plain HTML text).

Cloud Sessions (Copilot Coding Agent)

Runs on GitHub's cloud infrastructure.
Works on your repository remotely and can create pull requests with solutions.
You can monitor progress, provide follow-up instructions, and course-correct the agent.
Best for delegating implementation work while you continue coding locally.

Third-Party Sessions (OpenAI Codex, etc.)

Agents from other providers (OpenAI, Anthropic) integrated through VS Code extensions.
Managed through the same Agent Sessions view.
Sign in with your GitHub Copilot credentials (for supported integrations like Codex).


6. Delegation — Handing Work Between Agents
A powerful pattern in VS Code is planning locally, then delegating implementation to another agent.
How It Works

You discuss and plan a task in a local chat session where you have full codebase context.
When ready, click the "Delegate to coding agent" button in the Chat view.
All context from your chat conversation — including file references — is forwarded to the target agent (cloud, background, or third-party).
The original session is archived, and the delegated agent begins work.

The delegate button appears by default when you're working in a repo with the Copilot coding agent enabled. You can also delegate from the Plan agent flow and from untitled prompt files.
You can delegate to any agent, including CLI sessions and custom agents. The delegation menu shows all available agents.

7. The Plan Agent — Structured Task Planning
The Plan agent is a built-in agent designed to turn vague, lazy prompts into detailed implementation plans before any code is written.
Behavior

You give it a loose prompt like "add drag and drop."
Instead of immediately writing code, it asks clarifying questions: What page? What library? What scope?
You answer on separate lines (one answer per question).
When it has enough information, it generates a structured step-by-step plan.
It then offers "Handoff" options: proceed to implementation (switches to Agent mode), or open the full plan in the editor for review/editing.

Handoff Feature
Handoff is the transition point where a plan becomes action. When the Plan agent finishes, you see buttons to either let the Agent implement the plan or to open the plan document for manual review. The handoff preserves all context accumulated during planning.

8. Subagents — Context-Isolated Workers
Subagents address the problem of context confusion — the more you interact in a single session, the more context accumulates, and the more likely the AI is to get confused or lose track.
How They Work

Reference the #runSubagent tool in your prompt.
The main agent creates a prompt and hands it to an independent subagent.
The subagent gets only the context you explicitly send. It knows nothing about the rest of your main chat.
The subagent runs autonomously — no user interaction, no pause for feedback.
When it finishes, only its final result is returned to the main chat's context. All intermediate steps stay isolated.
Subagents have access to most of the same tools as the main chat.

Use Cases

Research and analysis that would bloat main context.
Deep dives into specific files or topics.
Parallel investigation of different approaches.

Example prompt:
Analyze the #file:api with #runSubagent and recommend the best 
authentication strategy for a web client consuming these endpoints.
In the UI, you can see a subagent running beneath the main agent action, with its own tool calls and model responses displayed.

9. Checkpoints — Time Travel for Sessions
Checkpoints let you roll back to a previous point in a chat session — both the conversation history and any workspace file changes.
How They Work

Enabled by default (toggleable via chat.checkpoints.enabled).
As the agent makes changes during a session, VS Code creates checkpoints at each significant step.
Selecting a checkpoint reverts workspace changes and the chat history to that point.
After restoring a checkpoint, you can redo the undone action — it's not destructive.
Complements source control by providing more immediate, session-aware rollback.

When to Use

The agent went down a wrong path and you want to rewind to a known-good state.
You want to try a different approach from the same starting point.
Multiple files were changed and you need to undo across all of them atomically.


10. Context System — What the AI Knows
Implicit Context (Automatic)
VS Code automatically includes in the AI's context:

The active file (currently open in the editor).
Your current selection (highlighted text).
The file name and its language.
When using agents, the agent autonomously decides whether additional context is needed and fetches it.

Explicit Context (# mentions)
You can explicitly attach context by typing # in the chat input:
MentionWhat It Adds#fileA specific file's contents#folderContents of a folder#codebaseBroader codebase search results#terminalSelectionSelected terminal output#fetchFetches a URL's contents#githubRepoReferences a GitHub repository#runSubagentInvokes a context-isolated subagent#problemsCurrent diagnostics/errors#selectionCurrent editor selection
You can also reference files from past Git commits, not just the current working tree.
Context Window Monitoring
The chat input box includes a context usage indicator — a visual display of how much of the model's context window has been consumed. This helps you know when it's time to start a fresh session.
Post-Approval for External Data
When agent tools pull in external data (from MCP servers, fetch, etc.), VS Code supports post-approval — you can review the data before it's used in the chat session, protecting against prompt injection attacks.

11. Session History & Recall
Browsing History

When you start a new session, the Chat view shows your most recent sessions by default.
Select "Show All Sessions" for the full history with search and filtering.
The list is scoped to your current workspace by default. Without a workspace open, it shows all sessions across workspaces.
Sessions are organized around agent sessions with a revamped session picker.

Session Restore Behavior

By default (as of v1.108+), the Chat panel opens empty on restart instead of restoring the previous session.
This is configurable via chat.restoreLastPanelSession.

Saving & Exporting

Use /savePrompt to save a conversation as a reusable prompt file. VS Code generates a generalized prompt from your conversation that can be reused or shared with your team.
Chat sessions can also be exported for sharing or archival.

Recent Session Recall (Experimental)
An experimental feature that lets you recall context from recent sessions, bridging the gap between isolated sessions.

12. Keyboard Shortcuts & Navigation
ShortcutActionCtrl+N / ⌘NNew chat sessionAlt+Ctrl+↑ / ⌥⌘↑Go to previous prompt in sessionAlt+Ctrl+↓ / ⌥⌘↓Go to next prompt in sessionAlt+Ctrl+PageUp / ⌥⌘PageUpGo to previous code blockAlt+Ctrl+PageDown / ⌥⌘PageDownGo to next code blockDeleteRemove attached context itemShift+F10Context menu on suggested prompts
Pro tip from the VS Code team: Change the workbench.action.chat.submit keybinding to Ctrl+Enter so you stop accidentally sending messages when you just want a new line.

13. The Welcome Page as Session Hub
VS Code's welcome page can be configured as your startup experience for agent sessions. It provides:

Recent agent sessions: Quick access to jump back into any recent conversation.
Embedded chat widget: Start new tasks immediately without opening a separate view.
Quick actions: Common tasks like "New Chat", "Open Recent Session", etc.
Suggested prompts: Right-click for a context menu with Edit Prompt File to customize.

Configure via setting workbench.startupEditor → agentSessionsWelcomePage.

14. MCP (Model Context Protocol) Integration
MCP governs how external tools are discovered, configured, and invoked within chat sessions. It has evolved rapidly:
Key MCP Features in Chat

Autostart: MCP servers can automatically start after configuration changes (chat.mcp.autostart) — no manual refresh.
Trust prompts: When an MCP server starts for the first time or after an update, VS Code prompts you to confirm trust before execution.
Client credentials flow: For servers that don't support Dynamic Client Registration, you can manually supply credentials.
MCP marketplace: An integrated marketplace for discovering and installing MCP tools.
Tool scaling: Improved filtering and grouping for users with 100+ tools enabled. Better tool selection to reduce agent confusion.
Fully qualified tool names: provider.toolName format to avoid naming conflicts between built-in tools and MCP server tools.
Resource previews: Richer display of MCP resources in the chat interface.
Fetch tool URL approval: Fine-grained approval — automatically approve fetches from specific URLs or domains.


15. Custom Agents (Formerly "Chat Modes")
What VS Code previously called "chat modes" are now called custom agents. These let you create your own specialized AI personas with specific roles, available tools, and model preferences.
How They Work

Define a custom agent as a configuration file (prompt file format).
Specify the system prompt, which tools the agent can use, and which model to use.
Custom agents appear in the agent selector dropdown alongside built-in agents.
They work everywhere — local chat, delegation to CLI, delegation to cloud agents.
Agents can be shared at the enterprise/organization level via the .github-private/agents folder.

Agent Skills (Experimental, v1.108+)
Agent Skills let you teach the coding agent new capabilities. VS Code automatically detects skills from .github/skills/ (or .claude/skills/ for backwards compatibility) and loads them into chat context only when relevant to the request. This is domain-specific knowledge injection — the agent only "learns" a skill when it's actually needed.
AGENTS.md Support
Nested AGENTS.md files in your repository provide agent-specific instructions per directory, similar to how .editorconfig works for editor settings.

16. Inline Chat — Chat Inside the Editor
Separate from the sidebar/editor chat, inline chat lets you invoke AI directly within your code editor at the cursor position.

Triggered by a keyboard shortcut or right-click menu.
A small input field appears inline in the editor.
The AI's response is rendered as a diff — you can accept or reject changes.
In v1.108+, the new inline chat UI is enabled by default for all users.
Files modified outside the current editor are automatically opened in a side editor group.


17. Accessibility

Chat responses stream live in Accessible View as they're generated.
The chat input announces the active agent and model in a clear order for screen readers.
MCP server output is excluded from Accessible View by default to reduce noise.
All attached context items can be managed via keyboard (Delete to remove).
Focus management follows screen reader conventions.


18. Settings Reference
SettingDescriptionchat.checkpoints.enabledEnable/disable session checkpoints (default: true)chat.restoreLastPanelSessionWhether to restore the last session on startupchat.math.enabledEnable KaTeX math rendering in chatchat.mcp.autostartAuto-start MCP servers after config changesworkbench.startupEditorSet to agentSessionsWelcomePage for session-focused startup

Summary — How It All Fits Together
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    VS Code Window                       │
│                                                         │
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐│
│  │ Agent        │  │  Editor Tabs                     ││
│  │ Sessions     │  │  ┌────────┬────────┬───────────┐ ││
│  │ View         │  │  │code.ts │chat    │ chat      │ ││
│  │              │  │  │        │editor 1│ editor 2  │ ││
│  │ ● Local #1   │  │  └────────┴────────┴───────────┘ ││
│  │   Running    │  │                                   ││
│  │ ● Cloud #2   │  │  Your code / Chat editor tabs     ││
│  │   Complete   │  │                                   ││
│  │ ● CLI #3     │  │  ┌─────────────────────────┐      ││
│  │   Queued     │  │  │  Inline Chat (in editor) │     ││
│  │ ● Codex #4   │  │  └─────────────────────────┘      ││
│  │   Running    │  │                                   ││
│  │              │  ├──────────────────────────────────┤│
│  │ [+ New]      │  │  Sidebar Chat                    ││
│  │              │  │  [Agent ▾] [Model ▾] [Context ▊]││
│  │              │  │  ┌──────────────────────────────┐││
│  │              │  │  │ Session messages              │││
│  │              │  │  │ User → Assistant → User → ... │││
│  │              │  │  │ Checkpoints ● ● ●            │││
│  │              │  │  └──────────────────────────────┘││
│  │              │  │  ┌──────────────────────────────┐││
│  │              │  │  │ #file #codebase  [Send ▾]    │││
│  │              │  │  │ Queue│Steer│Stop              │││
│  │              │  │  └──────────────────────────────┘││
│  └──────────────┘  └──────────────────────────────────┘│
│  Status Bar: [Agent #1: running] [Agent #4: running]    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
The system is designed around a core principle: you should be able to work with multiple AI agents simultaneously, each running in the right environment, without leaving your editor. Sessions are the unit of organization, the Agent Sessions view is the control center, and delegation + subagents let you distribute work across local, background, and cloud environments while keeping context clean.' and please now use this to inprove my chat window and also to improve my devlogs be nice if you can help me here please make me impressed

GitHub Copilot: Searched codebase for "within the chatwindow where chat messages are loaded, let it be so that if there is low count of messagaes aka message spawns far up top the screen try to make messages start at just aboue the chat and all new messages appeares at bottom of chat and pushes previous messages up smoothly and in empty chat you see default optioons to build or start a template or send a preset message etc.. menu is clean and text chat is moved to more center of window, as you send you first text chat window moves down and a message appears abouve it.. next message pushed the first message up neatly smooth and thats how I want it... I want messages that arrive and or are send to appear above the input so that we keep track of messages closes to where user has his eyes focues when writing... and like lets adopt features from claude or vscode 'Claude Chat Interface — Complete Replication Specification

Purpose: This document is a deep-dive, developer-ready prompt/specification for building a chat interface that mirrors the Claude.ai chat experience. It covers layout, styling, animations, responsive behavior, message lifecycle, input mechanics, scrolling, theming, and hidden UX details.


1. Global Layout Architecture
Desktop (≥ 1024px)
The page is a horizontally split layout:

Left sidebar (~260px wide, collapsible): Contains conversation history, a "New Chat" button, and user/settings at the bottom.
Main chat area (remaining width): A vertically stacked column — top bar, message thread, and input area.

The main chat column is centered and max-width constrained inside the viewport. The message content area has a max-width of roughly 48rem (768px) and is horizontally centered with margin: 0 auto. This prevents ultra-wide lines on large monitors and keeps readability optimal.
┌──────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Sidebar  │  Top Bar (model selector, controls)   │
│          ├───────────────────────────────────────┤
│ History  │                                       │
│ list     │     Message Thread (scrollable)        │
│          │     max-width: ~768px, centered        │
│          │                                       │
│          ├───────────────────────────────────────┤
│          │     Input Area (sticky bottom)         │
│ Settings └───────────────────────────────────────┘
└──────────┘
Tablet (768px–1023px)

Sidebar becomes an overlay drawer toggled by a hamburger icon.
Chat area takes full width.
Message content max-width drops slightly (~640px).

Mobile (< 768px)

Sidebar is fully hidden (overlay drawer accessible via hamburger).
Message bubbles go full-width minus small padding (16px each side).
Input area is fixed to the bottom, above the virtual keyboard.
Top bar simplifies: model selector may collapse into a dropdown or icon.


2. The Empty / Welcome State
When no messages exist yet (new conversation), the UI shows a centered welcome state instead of the message thread:

A greeting like "How can I help you today?" or similar, vertically and horizontally centered.
Optional quick-action suggestion chips below the greeting (e.g., "Write code", "Analyze data", "Brainstorm ideas").
The input area is still pinned at the bottom.
On first message send, the welcome state fades out and the message thread takes over.

Transition: welcome state → opacity: 0 over ~200ms → display: none → message thread mounts

3. The Input Area — Anatomy & Behavior
Structure
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│ │  [Attachment+] [textarea                     ]  │ │
│ │               auto-growing, placeholder text    │ │
│ │  [Tool toggles row]            [Send button ▶] │ │
│ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│   "Claude can make mistakes..." disclaimer text     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Textarea Mechanics

Element type: A <textarea> (or contenteditable div) — NOT a single-line <input>.
Auto-grow: The textarea starts at ~1 line (~44px height). As the user types and text wraps, it grows line-by-line up to a max-height of ~200px (roughly 8–10 lines). After that, internal scrolling kicks in.
Implementation:

javascript// On every input event:
textarea.style.height = 'auto';                     // Reset to shrink if needed
textarea.style.height = Math.min(textarea.scrollHeight, maxHeight) + 'px';

Placeholder text: Something like "Message Claude..." — disappears on focus or first character.
Line breaks: Shift+Enter inserts a newline. Plain Enter sends the message.
On send: The textarea resets to its initial 1-line height.

Send Button

Positioned at the bottom-right inside the input container.
Disabled state: When textarea is empty, the send icon is muted/grayed (pointer-events: none or reduced opacity).
Active state: When text is present, the button becomes a filled circle with an arrow-up icon, colored with the brand accent.
Transition: The color/opacity change is animated over ~150ms.
During streaming: The send button transforms into a stop button (square icon inside circle). Clicking it aborts the stream.

Attachment Button

A paperclip or "+" icon to the left of the textarea.
Opens a file picker or a small popover menu (upload file, paste image, etc.).
When a file is attached, a pill/chip appears above the textarea showing filename + remove "×" button.

Tool Toggles

Below the textarea (or in a row beneath it), small toggleable chips for features like "Search", "Artifacts", "Code execution."
These are subtle — small text, icon + label, toggle on/off with a background color change.

Disclaimer

Below the entire input box container: small, muted gray text: "Claude can make mistakes. Please double-check responses."
Font size ~11–12px, color: #9CA3AF (light mode) / #6B7280 (dark mode).


4. Sending a Message — Complete Lifecycle
Step-by-step flow:

User presses Enter (or clicks Send):

The textarea content is captured as a string.
The textarea is immediately cleared and reset to 1-line height.
The send button transitions to a stop button.


User message appears instantly:

A new message bubble is appended to the thread with the user's text.
The message thread auto-scrolls to the bottom (smooth scroll, ~300ms).
No loading delay — the user message renders immediately (optimistic UI).


Assistant "thinking" indicator:

Below the user message, an assistant message container appears with a typing/thinking indicator.
This is typically a pulsing dot animation or a shimmer bar.
Implementation: 3 dots with staggered animation-delay on a @keyframes opacity/translateY pulse.



css.typing-dot {
  animation: pulse 1.4s ease-in-out infinite;
}
.typing-dot:nth-child(2) { animation-delay: 0.2s; }
.typing-dot:nth-child(3) { animation-delay: 0.4s; }

@keyframes pulse {
  0%, 80%, 100% { opacity: 0.3; transform: translateY(0); }
  40% { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(-4px); }
}

Streaming response begins:

Text is appended token-by-token (or chunk-by-chunk) via Server-Sent Events (SSE) or WebSocket.
The thinking indicator is replaced by actual text.
Text appears with a subtle fade-in per token/word — each new chunk fades from opacity: 0 to 1 over ~100ms.
Markdown is parsed and rendered in real-time as tokens arrive (bold, code blocks, lists become formatted incrementally).
The thread auto-scrolls continuously during streaming IF the user was already at the bottom. If the user has manually scrolled up, auto-scroll is paused (see Scroll section).


Stream completes:

The stop button reverts to the send button.
Action buttons appear below the assistant message (copy, thumbs up/down, retry).
These buttons fade in over ~200ms.




5. Message Rendering — Anatomy & Styling
User Messages
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                              ┌─────────┐ │
│                              │ User msg │ │
│                              │ text     │ │
│                              └─────────┘ │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘

Alignment: Right-aligned (or full-width with a distinct background).
Background: A subtle tinted background — e.g., #F3F4F6 (light) or #2D2D2D (dark). Some implementations use a slightly different bg from assistant messages. Claude.ai uses a more minimal approach: user messages may just appear as plain text, right-aligned or with a subtle bg pill.
No avatar on user side (or a small user initial circle).
Max-width: ~80% of the content column to prevent full-width stretching of short messages.
Border-radius: 18px or 1.25rem — rounded pill shape.
Padding: 12px 16px.
Font: Same as body, ~15px, normal weight.

Assistant Messages
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ┌─────────┐                              │
│ │ Claude  │ ← small icon/avatar          │
│ │ logo    │                              │
│ └─────────┘                              │
│ Response text with **markdown** rendered  │
│ ```code blocks with syntax highlighting```│
│                                          │
│ [Copy] [👍] [👎] [Retry]                 │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘

Alignment: Left-aligned, full content width.
Avatar: Small Claude logo icon (sunburst/asterisk shape), ~24px, positioned top-left above or beside the message.
Background: Transparent or very subtle — assistant messages typically don't have a bubble background in Claude's UI. They flow as plain rendered markdown.
Markdown rendering: Full rich markdown:

Bold/italic via <strong> / <em>
Code blocks with syntax highlighting (use Prism.js, Highlight.js, or Shiki). Background: #1E1E1E (dark) or #F5F5F5 (light) with a copy button top-right.
Inline code with subtle background and monospace font.
Lists properly indented.
Tables with borders and alternating row shading.
LaTeX/math rendered via KaTeX or MathJax if applicable.
Links styled with brand color, underlined on hover.



Action Buttons (below assistant messages)

Appear only on the last assistant message (or on hover for older messages).
Icons: Copy (clipboard), Thumbs Up, Thumbs Down, Retry (circular arrow).
Style: Ghost buttons, ~28px icon size, muted color. On hover: slightly darker + subtle background circle.
Spacing: gap: 8px between them.
Visibility logic: Always visible on the latest response. On older messages, they appear on hover over the message area with a opacity: 0 → 1 transition (~150ms).


6. Scrolling Behavior — The Tricky Part
Auto-scroll Rules
This is one of the most important UX details and hardest to get right:

User sends a message → always auto-scroll to bottom.
During streaming:

If the user is at or near the bottom (within ~100px threshold), keep auto-scrolling as new content arrives.
If the user has scrolled up manually (more than ~100px from the bottom), stop auto-scrolling. Show a "↓ Scroll to bottom" floating button.


"Scroll to bottom" button:

Appears as a small circular FAB (floating action button) near the bottom-right of the message thread.
Clicking it smooth-scrolls to the bottom and re-enables auto-scroll.
Disappears when the user is at the bottom.



Implementation
javascriptconst container = messageThreadRef.current;
const isNearBottom = container.scrollHeight - container.scrollTop - container.clientHeight < 100;

// During streaming, on every new token:
if (isNearBottom) {
  container.scrollTo({ top: container.scrollHeight, behavior: 'smooth' });
}

// Show/hide scroll-to-bottom button:
setShowScrollButton(!isNearBottom);
Scroll Anchoring
When new content is prepended (e.g., loading older messages on scroll-up), use scroll anchoring to prevent the viewport from jumping:
javascriptconst previousScrollHeight = container.scrollHeight;
// ... prepend messages ...
const newScrollHeight = container.scrollHeight;
container.scrollTop += (newScrollHeight - previousScrollHeight);
Mobile Scroll Considerations

-webkit-overflow-scrolling: touch for momentum scrolling on iOS.
The message thread uses overflow-y: auto with flex: 1 to fill available space between top bar and input.
When the virtual keyboard opens on mobile, the input area should push up and the message thread should shrink. Use visualViewport API or dvh units:

css.chat-container {
  height: 100dvh; /* dynamic viewport height — accounts for mobile keyboard */
}

7. Theme System (Light / Dark / System)
Color Tokens
Define a semantic color system, not raw hex values:
css:root {
  /* Light theme */
  --bg-primary: #FFFFFF;
  --bg-secondary: #F9FAFB;
  --bg-tertiary: #F3F4F6;
  --bg-user-message: #F3F4F6;
  --bg-code-block: #F5F5F5;
  --bg-input: #FFFFFF;
  --bg-sidebar: #F9FAFB;

  --text-primary: #111827;
  --text-secondary: #6B7280;
  --text-tertiary: #9CA3AF;
  --text-on-accent: #FFFFFF;

  --border-primary: #E5E7EB;
  --border-input: #D1D5DB;
  --border-input-focus: #D97706;  /* warm accent on focus */

  --accent-primary: #D97706;      /* Claude's warm orange-brown */
  --accent-hover: #B45309;

  --shadow-sm: 0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,0.05);
  --shadow-md: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.07);
}

[data-theme="dark"] {
  --bg-primary: #1A1A1A;
  --bg-secondary: #212121;
  --bg-tertiary: #2D2D2D;
  --bg-user-message: #2D2D2D;
  --bg-code-block: #1E1E1E;
  --bg-input: #212121;
  --bg-sidebar: #171717;

  --text-primary: #F3F4F6;
  --text-secondary: #9CA3AF;
  --text-tertiary: #6B7280;

  --border-primary: #374151;
  --border-input: #4B5563;
  --border-input-focus: #F59E0B;

  --accent-primary: #F59E0B;
  --accent-hover: #D97706;

  --shadow-sm: 0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,0.3);
  --shadow-md: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.4);
}
Theme Switching Logic
javascriptfunction setTheme(mode) {
  // mode: 'light' | 'dark' | 'system'
  if (mode === 'system') {
    const prefersDark = window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)').matches;
    document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme', prefersDark ? 'dark' : 'light');
    // Listen for system changes:
    window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)')
      .addEventListener('change', e => {
        document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme', e.matches ? 'dark' : 'light');
      });
  } else {
    document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme', mode);
  }
  localStorage.setItem('theme-preference', mode);
}
Theme Transition
To prevent a jarring flash when toggling themes:
css* {
  transition: background-color 0.2s ease, color 0.2s ease, border-color 0.2s ease;
}

8. Sidebar — Conversation History
Structure
┌─────────────────────┐
│ [+ New Chat]        │  ← Primary action button
│─────────────────────│
│ 🔍 Search chats     │  ← Search input
│─────────────────────│
│ Today               │  ← Date group header
│  ○ Chat title 1     │
│  ○ Chat title 2     │
│ Yesterday           │
│  ○ Chat title 3     │
│ Previous 7 Days     │
│  ○ Chat title 4     │
│─────────────────────│
│ [User avatar] Name  │  ← Bottom: user info + settings
│ [Settings gear]     │
└─────────────────────┘
Behavior

New Chat button: Clears the message thread, resets to welcome state, focuses the input.
Chat items: Clicking loads that conversation. The active chat is highlighted with bg-tertiary + left border accent.
Chat title: Auto-generated from the first user message (first ~30 chars, truncated with ellipsis).
Hover actions: On hover over a chat item, show a "..." menu (rename, delete, share).
Sidebar collapse:

Desktop: A toggle arrow at the top collapses the sidebar to ~0px width. The chat area expands. Collapsed state shows only small icons.
Mobile: Sidebar is an overlay with backdrop blur/dim. Swiping left or tapping outside closes it.



Animation
css.sidebar {
  transition: width 0.25s cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
  overflow: hidden;
}
.sidebar.collapsed {
  width: 0;
}

9. Responsive Breakpoints & Adaptations
BreakpointSidebarMessage WidthInputTop Bar≥1280px (XL)Visible, 260pxmax-width: 768pxFull featuresFull1024–1279pxVisible, 240pxmax-width: 680pxFull featuresFull768–1023pxOverlay drawermax-width: 640pxSimplified togglesCondensed<768pxOverlay drawer100% - 32px paddingMinimal, full-widthMinimal
Mobile-Specific Adaptations

Input sticks above keyboard: Use position: fixed; bottom: 0 with dvh for height.
Touch-friendly tap targets: All buttons ≥ 44px tap area.
Swipe gestures: Swipe right from left edge opens sidebar.
Message long-press: On mobile, long-pressing a message shows a context menu (copy, retry) instead of hover actions.
Viewport meta tag:

html<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, maximum-scale=1, viewport-fit=cover">

Safe areas for notched phones:

css.input-area {
  padding-bottom: calc(12px + env(safe-area-inset-bottom));
}

10. Typography
cssbody {
  font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif;
  font-size: 15px;         /* Base size for messages */
  line-height: 1.6;        /* Comfortable reading */
  letter-spacing: -0.01em;
  -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
}

code, pre {
  font-family: 'SFMono-Regular', 'Fira Code', 'Fira Mono', 'Menlo', 'Consolas', monospace;
  font-size: 13.5px;
}

.chat-title { font-size: 14px; font-weight: 500; }
.timestamp  { font-size: 12px; color: var(--text-tertiary); }
.disclaimer { font-size: 12px; color: var(--text-tertiary); }

11. Hidden UX Details & Micro-interactions
Keyboard Shortcuts
ShortcutActionEnterSend messageShift+EnterNew line in inputCtrl/Cmd+Shift+NNew conversationCtrl/Cmd+Shift+CCopy last responseCtrl/Cmd+/Toggle sidebarEscapeStop streaming / Close modal↑ (when input empty)Edit last sent message
Input Focus Management

On page load → auto-focus the textarea.
After sending a message → re-focus the textarea.
After clicking "New Chat" → focus the textarea.
After closing any modal/dialog → return focus to textarea.
On mobile: don't auto-focus on page load (prevents keyboard from opening immediately).

Copy Code Block Button

Every fenced code block gets a "Copy" button in the top-right corner.
On click: copies code to clipboard, button text changes to "Copied!" with a check icon for ~2 seconds, then reverts.
Implementation:

javascriptasync function copyCode(text, button) {
  await navigator.clipboard.writeText(text);
  button.textContent = 'Copied!';
  setTimeout(() => { button.textContent = 'Copy'; }, 2000);
}
Message Edit (User Messages)

Hover over a sent user message → a small pencil icon appears.
Clicking it turns the message back into an editable textarea (pre-filled with original text).
Submitting re-sends the message, and all subsequent messages (assistant response + any follow-ups) are removed and regenerated.

Retry / Regenerate

The retry button on the last assistant message re-sends the same user prompt.
A new response streams in, replacing the previous one.
Some implementations keep a "carousel" of responses: ← 1/3 → navigation to browse through different generations.

Network Error Handling

If the stream disconnects mid-response:

Show a subtle error banner: "Connection lost. Trying to reconnect..."
Auto-retry with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s).
If unrecoverable: show a "Retry" button inline below the partial response.


If the send fails entirely:

The user message stays, but gets a red warning icon.
A "Retry" link appears: "Failed to send. [Retry]"



Optimistic Updates & Loading States

User message appears instantly (no round-trip wait).
If the server rejects it (rate limit, content filter), the message gets a red border and error tooltip.
During streaming: a subtle progress shimmer may appear on the assistant's avatar or at the top of the response area.

Link Previews & Special Rendering

URLs in messages become clickable links with target="_blank" and rel="noopener noreferrer".
Images pasted or uploaded render inline as thumbnails (click to expand in a lightbox).
File attachments show as download-ready pills with icon + filename + size.


12. Animations & Transitions Summary
ElementTriggerAnimationWelcome state → messagesFirst message sentFade out 200msNew message appearsMessage appendedFade-in + slide-up 200ms, translateY(8px) → 0Streaming textEach token/chunkOpacity 0 → 1 over 100msTyping indicatorWaiting for response3-dot pulse loop, 1.4sAction buttonsResponse completeFade in 200msSend → Stop buttonStream starts/endsCross-fade 150msSidebar open/closeToggleWidth 250ms ease-out (desktop), slide-in 300ms (mobile)Theme switchUser togglesBackground/color transitions 200msScroll-to-bottom FABScroll position changeOpacity + scale 200msCode "Copy" → "Copied!"Click copyText swap, no animation neededHover action buttonsMouse enter/leaveOpacity 150ms

13. Accessibility

All interactive elements have visible focus rings (outline: 2px solid var(--accent-primary) on :focus-visible).
ARIA roles: role="log" on message thread, role="status" on typing indicator.
aria-live="polite" on the message thread so screen readers announce new messages.
The send button has aria-label="Send message" (or "Stop generating" during streaming).
Color contrast ratios meet WCAG AA (4.5:1 for text, 3:1 for large text/icons).
Keyboard navigation: Tab moves between sidebar, input, and action buttons logically.


14. Data Flow Architecture (Frontend)
User Input
    │
    ▼
[Client State Manager (React state / Zustand / Redux)]
    │
    ├── messages: Array<{ id, role, content, timestamp, status }>
    ├── isStreaming: boolean
    ├── currentConversationId: string
    ├── theme: 'light' | 'dark' | 'system'
    └── sidebarOpen: boolean
    │
    ▼
[API Layer]
    │
    ├── POST /api/chat  → sends { messages, model, conversationId }
    │                     returns SSE stream of delta tokens
    │
    ├── GET /api/conversations → returns conversation list
    │
    └── DELETE /api/conversations/:id → deletes a conversation
    │
    ▼
[SSE / WebSocket Stream Handler]
    │
    ├── on 'delta' → append token to current assistant message
    ├── on 'done'  → finalize message, enable input
    ├── on 'error' → show error state, enable retry
    └── on 'close' → cleanup connection
Stream Handling (SSE)
javascriptasync function sendMessage(userMessage) {
  // 1. Optimistically add user message
  addMessage({ role: 'user', content: userMessage, status: 'sent' });

  // 2. Create placeholder for assistant
  const assistantMsgId = addMessage({ role: 'assistant', content: '', status: 'streaming' });

  // 3. Open SSE stream
  const response = await fetch('/api/chat', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify({ messages: getMessages(), model: selectedModel }),
    signal: abortController.signal,  // For stop button
  });

  const reader = response.body.getReader();
  const decoder = new TextDecoder();

  while (true) {
    const { done, value } = await reader.read();
    if (done) break;

    const chunk = decoder.decode(value);
    // Parse SSE format: "data: {...}\n\n"
    const lines = chunk.split('\n').filter(line => line.startsWith('data: '));

    for (const line of lines) {
      const data = JSON.parse(line.slice(6));
      if (data.type === 'content_block_delta') {
        appendToMessage(assistantMsgId, data.delta.text);
      }
    }
  }

  updateMessageStatus(assistantMsgId, 'complete');
}

15. Performance Optimizations

Virtualized message list: For long conversations (100+ messages), use a virtualized list (react-window or @tanstack/virtual) so only visible messages are in the DOM.
Debounced auto-resize: Debounce the textarea resize calculation by ~16ms (one frame) to prevent layout thrashing.
Memoized message components: Each message component is wrapped in React.memo with a comparator that checks content and status.
Lazy markdown parsing: Don't re-parse all messages on every render. Parse once on mount/content-change and cache the HTML output.
Code highlighting: Use a web worker or lazy-load the syntax highlighter only when code blocks are present.
Image lazy loading: loading="lazy" on all image elements.


16. The Complete CSS Reset & Base
css*, *::before, *::after {
  margin: 0;
  padding: 0;
  box-sizing: border-box;
}

html {
  height: 100%;
  -webkit-text-size-adjust: 100%;
}

body {
  height: 100%;
  background: var(--bg-primary);
  color: var(--text-primary);
  font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', sans-serif;
  overflow: hidden; /* The message thread scrolls, not the body */
}

/* Scrollbar styling */
::-webkit-scrollbar { width: 6px; }
::-webkit-scrollbar-track { background: transparent; }
::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb {
  background: var(--border-primary);
  border-radius: 3px;
}
::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb:hover { background: var(--text-tertiary); }

/* Firefox */
* {
  scrollbar-width: thin;
  scrollbar-color: var(--border-primary) transparent;
}

/* Selection color */
::selection {
  background: rgba(217, 119, 6, 0.25);  /* Brand accent with transparency */
}

src/
├── components/
│   ├── Chat/
│   │   ├── ChatContainer.jsx      # Main layout orchestrator
│   │   ├── MessageThread.jsx       # Scrollable message list

│   │   ├── MarkdownRenderer.jsx    # Renders markdown to HTML

│   │   ├── ScrollToBottom.jsx      # FAB button

│   ├── Input/
│   │   ├── ChatInput.jsx           # Textarea + send + attachments
│   │   ├── AutoGrowTextarea.jsx    # Self-resizing textarea
│   │   ├── SendButton.jsx          # Send/Stop toggle
│   │   ├── AttachmentButton.jsx    # File upload
│   │   └── ToolToggles.jsx         # Feature chips

│   │   └── UserMenu.jsx            # Bottom user/settings
│   └── shared/
│       ├── ThemeToggle.jsx
│       ├── Modal.jsx
│       └── Tooltip.jsx
├── hooks/
│   ├── useChat.js                  # Message state + streaming logic
│   ├── useAutoScroll.js            # Scroll position tracking

└── App.jsx

Summary Checklist

 Centered, max-width message column
 Auto-growing textarea with Enter to send, Shift+Enter for newline
 Optimistic user message rendering
 SSE streaming with token-by-token display
 Typing indicator with pulsing dots
 Smart auto-scroll with manual-scroll detection
 Scroll-to-bottom floating button
 Dark / Light / System theme with CSS variables
 Collapsible sidebar with conversation history
 Responsive: sidebar drawer on mobile, full-width messages
 100dvh layout for mobile keyboard handling
 Code blocks with syntax highlighting and copy button
 Message action buttons (copy, rate, retry) on hover / last message
 Send ↔ Stop button toggle during streaming
 Keyboard shortcuts
 Accessibility (ARIA, focus management, contrast)
 Virtualized list for long conversations
 Error handling with retry UI
 Safe area insets for notched devices
 Smooth transitions on all state changes' and or 'VS Code Chat & Agent Sessions — Complete Deep Dive

Purpose: A comprehensive breakdown of how VS Code's Chat and Agent Sessions system works — the UI surfaces, session lifecycle, agent orchestration, context management, checkpoints, message handling, and all the hidden mechanics that make it tick.


1. What Is a Chat Session?
A chat session in VS Code is a single, isolated conversation between you and an AI model. It includes every prompt you've sent, every response you've received, and all the context (files, symbols, terminal output) that was referenced during that conversation. Each session maintains its own context window — a running accumulation of everything the AI "remembers" within that conversation. When you start a new session, that context is wiped clean.
Key properties of a session:

Independent context: Session A knows nothing about Session B.
Persistent history: Even after starting a new session, old sessions are saved and can be reopened.
Movable across views: A session can live in the sidebar, an editor tab, or a separate window — and you can move it between these at any time without losing state.
Multi-agent aware: A single session can involve local agents, background agents, cloud agents, and third-party agents.


2. The Three Chat Surfaces
VS Code doesn't confine chat to one location. There are three distinct surfaces where a session can live, each suited to a different workflow.
Sidebar Chat (Default)

Lives in the left or right sidebar alongside the file explorer, source control, etc.
Best for keeping chat visible while you code.
Narrow by default (~300–400px). When you make the sidebar wider, it automatically switches to a side-by-side mode where the chat and your code share the panel.
Shortcut: Ctrl+N / ⌘N opens a new session here by default.

Editor Tab Chat

Opens the chat as a full editor tab, just like a code file.
Best for giving chat more space, comparing sessions side by side (split tabs), or when you want chat to behave like a document.
Shortcut: New Chat (+) → New Chat Editor or the command Chat: New Chat Editor.

Separate Window Chat

Opens chat in an entirely separate OS window.
Best for multi-monitor setups — chat on one screen, code on the other.
Shortcut: New Chat (+) → New Chat Window.

You can move an existing session between any of these surfaces at any time via the ... menu on the chat or the Command Palette (Chat: Move Chat into Editor Area, Chat: Move Chat into Side Bar, Chat: Move Chat into New Window). The full conversation history and context are preserved during the move.

3. Anatomy of the Chat UI
The Input Area
The chat input box is a multi-line text field at the bottom of the chat surface. It's more than a simple textarea — it's context-aware and interactive.
Components inside/around the input:

Agent selector dropdown (top-left of input or above it): Lets you switch between built-in agents (Agent, Plan, Ask) or any custom agents you've created. This determines the AI's persona and capabilities for the session.
Model picker (near the agent selector): Choose which language model powers the session (GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet, Gemini, etc.). You can customize which models appear here.
Context indicator: Shows a visual representation of how much of the context window has been consumed. This fills up as the conversation grows. When it's nearly full, starting a new session is recommended.
# mentions: Typing # in the input opens a picker for explicitly attaching context — files (#file), folders, symbols, your entire codebase (#codebase), terminal output (#terminalSelection), tools like #fetch, #githubRepo, and special tools like #runSubagent.
@ mentions: Typing @ invokes specialized chat participants like @vscode, @terminal, or @workspace.
Attached context chips: When you reference files or context, they appear as removable chips/pills above or within the input. Press Delete to remove them.

Send Button Behavior
The send button is not just a simple "send." During an active response, it transforms into a dropdown with three options (experimental feature called message steering and queuing):

Add to Queue: Your message waits and sends automatically after the current response finishes.
Steer with Message: Signals the current request to yield after its current tool execution, then processes your new message immediately — essentially course-correcting the agent mid-run.
Stop and Send: Cancels the current request entirely and sends your new message.

When you have multiple pending messages (queued or steering), you can drag and drop them to reorder the queue. A drag handle appears on hover when multiple messages are pending.
Message Rendering

User messages: Appear as plain text blocks, right-aligned or in a distinct style. They support inline editing — click the edit icon (pencil) on any previous user message to revise and resend it. This is now the default behavior (inline editing), not a separate mode.
Assistant messages: Rendered with full markdown — code blocks with syntax highlighting, tables, math (KaTeX via chat.math.enabled), links, and structured output. Tool calls and their results are shown as collapsible sections with summarized titles.
Chain-of-thought display: When the model reasons internally, those reasoning steps can be shown as collapsible sections within the response.
Task list tracking (experimental): The AI can generate task breakdowns that show real-time progress within the chat — checkboxes that update as the agent completes steps.


4. The Agent Sessions View ("Agent HQ")
This is the centerpiece of VS Code's multi-agent orchestration. It's a dedicated sidebar view called "Agent Sessions" that acts as mission control for all your AI agents.
What It Shows

All active and past sessions across every agent type: local Copilot, Copilot coding agent (cloud), GitHub Copilot CLI, OpenAI Codex, and any third-party agents.
Session status: Running, completed, waiting for input, errored.
Rich descriptions: Each session shows contextual details about what it's doing.
Progress info: For running sessions, it shows the last tool call's progress instead of just a generic "working..." message.
Changed files and associated PRs: For cloud agents, you can see which files were modified and any pull requests created.
Grouping: Sessions are grouped by state and age when displayed side by side.

Session Management Actions

"+" button in the view header to quickly start a new session.
Context menu (right-click): Archive, mark as read/unread, open, delete.
Keyboard access: Archive, change read state, and open sessions without touching the mouse.
Batch operations: Archive multiple sessions at once from group sections.
Status bar tracking: Monitor multiple running agents from the VS Code status bar.

Unified Single-View Mode
In the latest iterations, the Agent Sessions view supports a unified single-view mode that displays all sessions from different providers in one place, with filtering, searching, and session-state management at a glance. No more switching between separate views for Copilot vs. Codex vs. CLI.

5. Session Types — Where the AI Runs
When you create a session, you're choosing not just what agent to use, but where it runs. VS Code supports four session environments:
Local Sessions

The AI runs within your VS Code instance on your machine.
Has direct access to your workspace files, terminal, and extensions.
Best for interactive, real-time coding assistance.

Background Sessions (formerly CLI Sessions)

Runs in a separate process using the GitHub Copilot CLI.
Can run in an isolated Git worktree — VS Code spins up a dedicated worktree per session so file changes live in a separate folder. Multiple background agents can work simultaneously without conflicting edits.
You can review and merge worktree changes back into the main workspace, or apply them directly when the agent completes.
Accepts rich attached context: selections, problems, symbols, search results, and more.
Terminal output is rendered using an embedded xterm.js terminal instance with full ANSI color support (not plain HTML text).

Cloud Sessions (Copilot Coding Agent)

Runs on GitHub's cloud infrastructure.
Works on your repository remotely and can create pull requests with solutions.
You can monitor progress, provide follow-up instructions, and course-correct the agent.
Best for delegating implementation work while you continue coding locally.

Third-Party Sessions (OpenAI Codex, etc.)

Agents from other providers (OpenAI, Anthropic) integrated through VS Code extensions.
Managed through the same Agent Sessions view.
Sign in with your GitHub Copilot credentials (for supported integrations like Codex).


6. Delegation — Handing Work Between Agents
A powerful pattern in VS Code is planning locally, then delegating implementation to another agent.
How It Works

You discuss and plan a task in a local chat session where you have full codebase context.
When ready, click the "Delegate to coding agent" button in the Chat view.
All context from your chat conversation — including file references — is forwarded to the target agent (cloud, background, or third-party).
The original session is archived, and the delegated agent begins work.

The delegate button appears by default when you're working in a repo with the Copilot coding agent enabled. You can also delegate from the Plan agent flow and from untitled prompt files.
You can delegate to any agent, including CLI sessions and custom agents. The delegation menu shows all available agents.

7. The Plan Agent — Structured Task Planning
The Plan agent is a built-in agent designed to turn vague, lazy prompts into detailed implementation plans before any code is written.
Behavior

You give it a loose prompt like "add drag and drop."
Instead of immediately writing code, it asks clarifying questions: What page? What library? What scope?
You answer on separate lines (one answer per question).
When it has enough information, it generates a structured step-by-step plan.
It then offers "Handoff" options: proceed to implementation (switches to Agent mode), or open the full plan in the editor for review/editing.

Handoff Feature
Handoff is the transition point where a plan becomes action. When the Plan agent finishes, you see buttons to either let the Agent implement the plan or to open the plan document for manual review. The handoff preserves all context accumulated during planning.

8. Subagents — Context-Isolated Workers
Subagents address the problem of context confusion — the more you interact in a single session, the more context accumulates, and the more likely the AI is to get confused or lose track.
How They Work

Reference the #runSubagent tool in your prompt.
The main agent creates a prompt and hands it to an independent subagent.
The subagent gets only the context you explicitly send. It knows nothing about the rest of your main chat.
The subagent runs autonomously — no user interaction, no pause for feedback.
When it finishes, only its final result is returned to the main chat's context. All intermediate steps stay isolated.
Subagents have access to most of the same tools as the main chat.

Use Cases

Research and analysis that would bloat main context.
Deep dives into specific files or topics.
Parallel investigation of different approaches.

Example prompt:
Analyze the #file:api with #runSubagent and recommend the best 
authentication strategy for a web client consuming these endpoints.
In the UI, you can see a subagent running beneath the main agent action, with its own tool calls and model responses displayed.

9. Checkpoints — Time Travel for Sessions
Checkpoints let you roll back to a previous point in a chat session — both the conversation history and any workspace file changes.
How They Work

Enabled by default (toggleable via chat.checkpoints.enabled).
As the agent makes changes during a session, VS Code creates checkpoints at each significant step.
Selecting a checkpoint reverts workspace changes and the chat history to that point.
After restoring a checkpoint, you can redo the undone action — it's not destructive.
Complements source control by providing more immediate, session-aware rollback.

When to Use

The agent went down a wrong path and you want to rewind to a known-good state.
You want to try a different approach from the same starting point.
Multiple files were changed and you need to undo across all of them atomically.


10. Context System — What the AI Knows
Implicit Context (Automatic)
VS Code automatically includes in the AI's context:

The active file (currently open in the editor).
Your current selection (highlighted text).
The file name and its language.
When using agents, the agent autonomously decides whether additional context is needed and fetches it.

Explicit Context (# mentions)
You can explicitly attach context by typing # in the chat input:
MentionWhat It Adds#fileA specific file's contents#folderContents of a folder#codebaseBroader codebase search results#terminalSelectionSelected terminal output#fetchFetches a URL's contents#githubRepoReferences a GitHub repository#runSubagentInvokes a context-isolated subagent#problemsCurrent diagnostics/errors#selectionCurrent editor selection
You can also reference files from past Git commits, not just the current working tree.
Context Window Monitoring
The chat input box includes a context usage indicator — a visual display of how much of the model's context window has been consumed. This helps you know when it's time to start a fresh session.
Post-Approval for External Data
When agent tools pull in external data (from MCP servers, fetch, etc.), VS Code supports post-approval — you can review the data before it's used in the chat session, protecting against prompt injection attacks.

11. Session History & Recall
Browsing History

When you start a new session, the Chat view shows your most recent sessions by default.
Select "Show All Sessions" for the full history with search and filtering.
The list is scoped to your current workspace by default. Without a workspace open, it shows all sessions across workspaces.
Sessions are organized around agent sessions with a revamped session picker.

Session Restore Behavior

By default (as of v1.108+), the Chat panel opens empty on restart instead of restoring the previous session.
This is configurable via chat.restoreLastPanelSession.

Saving & Exporting

Use /savePrompt to save a conversation as a reusable prompt file. VS Code generates a generalized prompt from your conversation that can be reused or shared with your team.
Chat sessions can also be exported for sharing or archival.

Recent Session Recall (Experimental)
An experimental feature that lets you recall context from recent sessions, bridging the gap between isolated sessions.

12. Keyboard Shortcuts & Navigation
ShortcutActionCtrl+N / ⌘NNew chat sessionAlt+Ctrl+↑ / ⌥⌘↑Go to previous prompt in sessionAlt+Ctrl+↓ / ⌥⌘↓Go to next prompt in sessionAlt+Ctrl+PageUp / ⌥⌘PageUpGo to previous code blockAlt+Ctrl+PageDown / ⌥⌘PageDownGo to next code blockDeleteRemove attached context itemShift+F10Context menu on suggested prompts
Pro tip from the VS Code team: Change the workbench.action.chat.submit keybinding to Ctrl+Enter so you stop accidentally sending messages when you just want a new line.

13. The Welcome Page as Session Hub
VS Code's welcome page can be configured as your startup experience for agent sessions. It provides:

Recent agent sessions: Quick access to jump back into any recent conversation.
Embedded chat widget: Start new tasks immediately without opening a separate view.
Quick actions: Common tasks like "New Chat", "Open Recent Session", etc.
Suggested prompts: Right-click for a context menu with Edit Prompt File to customize.

Configure via setting workbench.startupEditor → agentSessionsWelcomePage.

14. MCP (Model Context Protocol) Integration
MCP governs how external tools are discovered, configured, and invoked within chat sessions. It has evolved rapidly:
Key MCP Features in Chat

Autostart: MCP servers can automatically start after configuration changes (chat.mcp.autostart) — no manual refresh.
Trust prompts: When an MCP server starts for the first time or after an update, VS Code prompts you to confirm trust before execution.
Client credentials flow: For servers that don't support Dynamic Client Registration, you can manually supply credentials.
MCP marketplace: An integrated marketplace for discovering and installing MCP tools.
Tool scaling: Improved filtering and grouping for users with 100+ tools enabled. Better tool selection to reduce agent confusion.
Fully qualified tool names: provider.toolName format to avoid naming conflicts between built-in tools and MCP server tools.
Resource previews: Richer display of MCP resources in the chat interface.
Fetch tool URL approval: Fine-grained approval — automatically approve fetches from specific URLs or domains.


15. Custom Agents (Formerly "Chat Modes")
What VS Code previously called "chat modes" are now called custom agents. These let you create your own specialized AI personas with specific roles, available tools, and model preferences.
How They Work

Define a custom agent as a configuration file (prompt file format).
Specify the system prompt, which tools the agent can use, and which model to use.
Custom agents appear in the agent selector dropdown alongside built-in agents.
They work everywhere — local chat, delegation to CLI, delegation to cloud agents.
Agents can be shared at the enterprise/organization level via the .github-private/agents folder.

Agent Skills (Experimental, v1.108+)
Agent Skills let you teach the coding agent new capabilities. VS Code automatically detects skills from .github/skills/ (or .claude/skills/ for backwards compatibility) and loads them into chat context only when relevant to the request. This is domain-specific knowledge injection — the agent only "learns" a skill when it's actually needed.
AGENTS.md Support
Nested AGENTS.md files in your repository provide agent-specific instructions per directory, similar to how .editorconfig works for editor settings.

16. Inline Chat — Chat Inside the Editor
Separate from the sidebar/editor chat, inline chat lets you invoke AI directly within your code editor at the cursor position.

Triggered by a keyboard shortcut or right-click menu.
A small input field appears inline in the editor.
The AI's response is rendered as a diff — you can accept or reject changes.
In v1.108+, the new inline chat UI is enabled by default for all users.
Files modified outside the current editor are automatically opened in a side editor group.


17. Accessibility

Chat responses stream live in Accessible View as they're generated.
The chat input announces the active agent and model in a clear order for screen readers.
MCP server output is excluded from Accessible View by default to reduce noise.
All attached context items can be managed via keyboard (Delete to remove).
Focus management follows screen reader conventions.


18. Settings Reference
SettingDescriptionchat.checkpoints.enabledEnable/disable session checkpoints (default: true)chat.restoreLastPanelSessionWhether to restore the last session on startupchat.math.enabledEnable KaTeX math rendering in chatchat.mcp.autostartAuto-start MCP servers after config changesworkbench.startupEditorSet to agentSessionsWelcomePage for session-focused startup

Summary — How It All Fits Together
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    VS Code Window                       │
│                                                         │
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐│
│  │ Agent        │  │  Editor Tabs                     ││
│  │ Sessions     │  │  ┌────────┬────────┬───────────┐ ││
│  │ View         │  │  │code.ts │chat    │ chat      │ ││
│  │              │  │  │        │editor 1│ editor 2  │ ││
│  │ ● Local #1   │  │  └────────┴────────┴───────────┘ ││
│  │   Running    │  │                                   ││
│  │ ● Cloud #2   │  │  Your code / Chat editor tabs     ││
│  │   Complete   │  │                                   ││
│  │ ● CLI #3     │  │  ┌─────────────────────────┐      ││
│  │   Queued     │  │  │  Inline Chat (in editor) │     ││
│  │ ● Codex #4   │  │  └─────────────────────────┘      ││
│  │   Running    │  │                                   ││
│  │              │  ├──────────────────────────────────┤│
│  │ [+ New]      │  │  Sidebar Chat                    ││
│  │              │  │  [Agent ▾] [Model ▾] [Context ▊]││
│  │              │  │  ┌──────────────────────────────┐││
│  │              │  │  │ Session messages              │││
│  │              │  │  │ User → Assistant → User → ... │││
│  │              │  │  │ Checkpoints ● ● ●            │││
│  │              │  │  └──────────────────────────────┘││
│  │              │  │  ┌──────────────────────────────┐││
│  │              │  │  │ #file #codebase  [Send ▾]    │││
│  │              │  │  │ Queue│Steer│Stop              │││
│  │              │  │  └──────────────────────────────┘││
│  └──────────────┘  └──────────────────────────────────┘│
│  Status Bar: [Agent #1: running] [Agent #4: running]    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
The system is designed around a core principle: you should be able to work with multiple AI agents simultaneously, each running in the right environment, without leaving your editor. Sessions are the unit of organization, the Agent Sessions view is the control center, and delegation + subagents let you distribute work across local, background, and cloud environments while keeping context clean.' and please now use this to inprove my chat window and also to improve my devlogs be nice if you can help me here please make me impressed", 24 results
