Here's what was built — a full Claude-inspired chat rewrite:

### New files created
- hooks/useAutoScroll.ts — Smart auto-scroll with near-bottom detection (100px threshold), pause on manual scroll-up, re-enable on FAB click, 50ms interval scroll-tracking during streaming
- components/ScrollToBottom.tsx — Animated FAB with framer-motion scale/opacity transitions, appears when user scrolls away
- components/TypingIndicator.tsx — 3-dot pulse animation with staggered delays (200ms), Bot avatar, fade-in via framer-motion

### Rewritten files
- **ChatWindow.tsx** — Complete Claude-style layout:
  - `flex-col justify-end` on message container — messages sit at the **bottom** when sparse, pushing up as they accumulate
  - Welcome state: centered "What shall we think through?" with Sparkles icon, preset cards, quick-action chips
  - On first message: welcome fades out (framer-motion), messages appear above input
  - Auto-growing textarea (1 line → max ~200px) instead of fixed-height input
  - Rounded 2xl input box with subtle shadow + focus ring
  - Paperclip + mode selector in bottom toolbar
  - Disclaimer text below input: "Vai can make mistakes. Verify important information."
  - No draggable divider — replaced with subtle gradient separator (cleaner, like Claude)
  - `AnimatePresence` wrapping message list for smooth mount/unmount animations

- **MessageBubble.tsx** — Claude-inspired message rendering:
  - User messages: right-aligned with `bg-zinc-800/70` pill, 85% max-width
  - Assistant messages: left-aligned, **no bubble** — flowing markdown (like Claude)
  - Action buttons (Copy, ThumbsUp, ThumbsDown, Retry) shown **below** the message, always visible on latest, hover-reveal on older
  - Streaming cursor: blinking `▍` character appended during stream
  - `isLatest` + `isStreaming` props for conditional behavior

- **index.css** — Enhanced with:
  - `typing-pulse` keyframe for 3-dot indicator
  - `cursor-blink` keyframe for streaming cursor
  - Firefox scrollbar support (`scrollbar-width: thin`)
  - `::selection` with violet tint
  - Table styling for markdown
  - Link hover colors
  - Code block borders

Made changes.

User: lets focus mainly on VS Code is the gold standard for sidebar architecture and features and The Sidebar Wars: Claude.ai vs VS Code vs Everyone Else

A deep comparison of how Claude.ai, VS Code, and other tools have designed their sidebars — and my honest take on what works, what doesn't, and who's doing it best.


Part 1: Claude.ai's Sidebar
What It Is
Claude.ai uses a classic two-column layout: a left sidebar for conversation history and a main chat area on the right. It's the same general pattern as ChatGPT, Gemini, and most consumer AI chat apps — but the details differ.
Anatomy
┌──────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┐
│ [≡ Claude logo]  │                                 │
│                  │                                 │
│ [+ New Chat]     │     Main Chat Area              │
│                  │     (centered, max-width)       │
│ [🔍 Search]      │                                 │
│──────────────────│                                 │
│ Today            │                                 │
│  ○ Chat title    │                                 │
│  ○ Chat title    │                                 │
│ Yesterday        │                                 │
│  ○ Chat title    │                                 │
│ Previous 7 Days  │                                 │
│  ○ Chat title    │                                 │
│──────────────────│                                 │
│ ⭐ Starred       │                                 │
│ 📁 Projects      │                                 │
│──────────────────│                                 │
│                  │                                 │
│ [User] Settings  │     Input Area (bottom)         │
└──────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘
Design Philosophy
Minimalist and conversation-first. The sidebar exists almost entirely to serve history navigation. It's not trying to be a tool panel, a settings hub, or a workspace manager. It's a flat list of past chats organized by time, with a few organizational features layered on top.
What's In It

New Chat button: Prominent, at the top. Clears everything and starts fresh.
Search: Lets you search across conversation history by keyword. Not a global search — it's scoped to your chat titles and content.
Conversation list: Grouped chronologically — Today, Yesterday, Previous 7 Days, Previous 30 Days, then by month. Each item shows a truncated title (auto-generated from the first message, or renamed by you).
Hover actions: Hovering a chat item reveals a ... menu with rename, delete, star, and share options.
Projects: A grouping mechanism. You can organize related conversations into projects with shared instructions and knowledge files. Projects appear as a separate section or navigable area.
Starred chats: A way to bookmark important conversations for quick access.
User menu / Settings: At the bottom — your profile, plan info, preferences, theme toggle.

Styling

Width: ~260px, fixed on desktop. Not user-resizable (no drag handle).
Background: Slightly off-white or light gray in light mode; dark charcoal in dark mode. Subtly distinct from the main chat area.
Typography: 14px, medium weight for chat titles. 12px, muted color for date group headers.
Active state: The current chat has a slightly highlighted background (subtle blue/gray tint) and possibly a left border accent.
Scrollbar: Thin, auto-hiding. Standard OS scrollbar or a thin custom one.
Transitions: Sidebar collapses to zero width on mobile (overlay drawer), triggered by hamburger icon. No intermediate "icon-only" state.

What It Doesn't Do

No tabs, no multi-panel views.
No file tree, terminal, or workspace tools.
No drag-and-drop reordering of conversations.
No "pin to top" beyond starring.
No side-by-side session comparison.
No visual indicators of conversation length, model used, or token count.

Mobile Behavior
On mobile, the sidebar disappears entirely and becomes an overlay drawer that slides in from the left, triggered by the hamburger menu. It takes the full height of the screen with the same content, just touch-optimized with larger tap targets. Swiping left or tapping outside dismisses it.

Part 2: VS Code's Sidebar
What It Is
VS Code doesn't have "a sidebar" — it has a sidebar system. There are actually three distinct sidebar-like surfaces, plus the Activity Bar that controls them. This is a fundamentally different architectural approach from any chat app.
The Architecture
┌──┬────────────────┬──────────────────────┬────────────────┐
│AB│ Primary        │                      │ Secondary      │
│  │ Sidebar        │   Editor Area        │ Sidebar        │
│  │                │                      │                │
│📁│ Explorer       │   code.ts            │ Chat View      │
│🔍│ File tree      │                      │ Agent selector │
│🔀│ with folders   │   Your actual code   │ Messages       │
│🐛│ and files      │                      │ Input box      │
│🧩│                │                      │                │
│💬│ Timeline       │                      │ Agent Sessions │
│  │ Outline        │                      │                │
│  ├────────────────┤                      ├────────────────┤
│  │ (draggable,    │   (split into tabs   │ (draggable,    │
│  │  resizable)    │    and groups)       │  resizable)    │
│──┴────────────────┴──────────────────────┴────────────────┘
│ Status Bar                                                 │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
The Activity Bar
This is the narrow vertical strip of icons on the far left (or right, if repositioned). Each icon represents a View Container — clicking it switches what the Primary Sidebar shows. Default icons include:

📁 Explorer (file tree)
🔍 Search
🔀 Source Control
🐛 Debug/Run
🧩 Extensions
💬 Chat (Copilot)
Plus any extension-contributed icons

The Activity Bar is tightly coupled to the Primary Sidebar. It's essentially a tab bar rotated 90 degrees.
Primary Sidebar

Default position: Left side.
Contains: Whichever View Container is active — Explorer, Search, Source Control, etc.
Each View Container can hold multiple Views stacked vertically. For example, the Explorer container holds the file tree, Timeline, and Outline views.
Resizable: Drag the edge to make it wider or narrower.
Collapsible: Toggle with Ctrl+B. Collapses to zero width (the Activity Bar remains visible).
User-customizable: You can drag views between containers, reorder them, and hide them.

Secondary Sidebar

Position: Opposite side of the Primary Sidebar (usually right).
Purpose: Originally created so you could see two sidebar surfaces at once — e.g., file tree on the left, chat on the right.
Default content: As of recent versions, the Chat view lives here by default.
Same capabilities: Supports View Containers, Views, resizing, collapsing.
Toggle: Ctrl+Alt+B.

The Chat Inside the Sidebar
The Copilot Chat view (or Claude Code panel, or any AI extension's chat) lives as a View within the sidebar system. It's not a separate app — it's a view that can be dragged to the Primary Sidebar, Secondary Sidebar, editor area, or even a floating window.
The chat view itself contains:

Agent/model selector at the top
Message thread (scrollable)
Input area with #-mentions, @-mentions, context attachments
Session picker dropdown for switching between past sessions

What Makes VS Code's Approach Unique

Everything is a view. Chat, file explorer, terminal, source control — they're all the same kind of UI element and can be rearranged freely.
Dual sidebars. You can literally have the file tree on the left AND chat on the right simultaneously, without tabs or switching.
Views compose. You can stack multiple views in one sidebar section, group them, split them.
The Activity Bar is a global switcher. It's always visible and provides one-click access to any major feature.
Extensions plug in. Any extension can add its own View Container to the Activity Bar and Views to the sidebars.


Part 3: How Other Players Do It
ChatGPT
Very similar to Claude.ai — left sidebar with conversation history, main chat area. Key differences:

GPT Store / Explore GPTs lives in the sidebar as a primary navigation item.
Conversations have auto-generated titles and can be organized into folders (relatively new).
The sidebar includes links to Canvas, image generation, and other tool modes.
On desktop, there's a persistent sidebar; on mobile, it's a slide-out drawer.
Overall feel is slightly more "product-y" than Claude — more buttons, more navigation items beyond just chat history.

Cursor (AI IDE)
Cursor is built on VS Code's core, so it inherits the same Activity Bar + Sidebar system. But it adds:

Chat sidebar (invoked with Cmd+I) as a primary surface.
Composer as a separate multi-file editing surface.
Agent mode that integrates into the chat sidebar.
The sidebar can reference your codebase, images, and web results directly.
It enforces a "chat-sidebar + inline-diff" workflow.

Gemini (Google)

Similar two-column layout.
Sidebar has Gems (custom personas) as a navigation concept alongside history.
Google-style clean Material Design.
Deeper integration with Google Workspace (Docs, Drive, Gmail) shows up in the sidebar.

Poe (Quora)

More colorful, busier sidebar.
Each bot/model has its own icon and entry point.
The sidebar is organized by bots first, history second.
Supports group chats and bot comparisons as first-class sidebar items.

Sider AI

Browser sidebar overlay (Chrome extension).
Appears as a slide-out panel on the right side of any webpage.
Contains chat, summarize, key points, and selection tools.
Multi-model: switch between ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini within the same sidebar.


Part 4: My Honest Analysis
Claude.ai's Sidebar: Clean but Underpowered
What it gets right:

The minimalism is intentional and works for casual users. You open Claude, you chat. The sidebar doesn't compete for attention.
Chronological grouping with search is sufficient for finding past conversations.
Projects give a level of organization that ChatGPT only recently matched with folders.
The mobile slide-out drawer is seamless.

What it gets wrong:

No resizing. The sidebar has a fixed width. If your chat titles are long, they just truncate. You can't drag it wider.
No rich metadata. You can't see at a glance which model a conversation used, how long it was, or whether it has attachments. It's just a title and a date.
No intermediate collapsed state. It's either full sidebar or completely hidden. There's no "icon-only" rail mode that shows just a thin strip of recent chats.
No multi-session awareness. You can only have one conversation active at a time. There's no concept of tabs or split-view for comparing two chats.
Projects feel bolted on. They're useful but feel separate from the main history flow. Navigating in and out of projects adds clicks.
No keyboard-driven navigation. You can't arrow-key through your conversation list or use keyboard shortcuts to jump to starred items.

VS Code's Sidebar: Powerful but Complex
What it gets right:

Flexibility is unmatched. The dual sidebar + Activity Bar + draggable views system means every developer can build their ideal layout. Chat on the right, files on the left, terminal at the bottom — all visible simultaneously.
The Activity Bar is brilliant. A vertical icon rail that provides persistent, one-click access to completely different functional domains. It's visually clean but incredibly dense with functionality.
Views as a primitive. Making everything — chat, file tree, source control, extensions — the same kind of composable UI element was a foundational design decision that pays dividends with every new feature.
Secondary Sidebar was prescient. They added it in 2022 and it turned out to be exactly what AI chat needed. Having chat on one side and code on the other is the killer layout for 2025.
Agent Sessions view. The unified dashboard for managing multiple concurrent AI agents is something no other tool has at this level.

What it gets wrong:

Overwhelming for newcomers. The first time you open VS Code with Copilot, extensions, source control, and debugger all adding icons to the Activity Bar and views to the sidebar, it's a lot. The flexibility creates cognitive overhead.
Chat is a second-class citizen spatially. Despite being one of the most-used features in 2025, chat is just another view squeezed into the same sidebar system as the file explorer. It works, but it doesn't feel designed for chat — it feels like chat was fit into an existing box.
No global session awareness in the sidebar. The Agent Sessions view is great but it's just another view you have to navigate to. There's no persistent visual indicator showing "you have 3 agents running" unless you check the status bar.
The sidebar can become a junk drawer. With extensions all contributing their own views, it's easy to end up with a sidebar that has 8+ views stacked vertically, most of which you never use.

Who's Doing It Best?
For a consumer chat product (like Claude.ai, ChatGPT):
I think Claude.ai's approach is correct but incomplete. The minimalist sidebar works because the product is focused — you're here to chat, and the sidebar helps you find past chats. But it needs:

A thin collapsed "rail" mode — like the Activity Bar concept, but just showing recent chat avatars/icons. This preserves screen space without fully hiding the sidebar.
Richer metadata on hover — model used, message count, date, whether it has artifacts.
Pinnable/tabbed sessions — the ability to have 2–3 chats accessible as tabs without navigating the full history.
Keyboard navigation — arrow keys through the list, Ctrl+K for quick-switch.

For a developer tool (like VS Code, Cursor):
VS Code is the gold standard for sidebar architecture, full stop. The Activity Bar + dual sidebar + composable views system is the most flexible sidebar design in any application I'm aware of. Cursor benefits from inheriting this, and Claude Code's VS Code extension wisely plugs into it rather than fighting it.
But the winner for best chat-in-sidebar experience is actually Cursor, because it was purpose-built around the chat-sidebar workflow. The chat isn't an afterthought jammed into the Explorer sidebar — it's the primary interaction surface, and everything else (file context, inline diffs, composer) flows from it.
The dark horse: Sider AI and the browser sidebar category. The idea of having AI chat as a persistent overlay on any webpage — not confined to a specific app — is underrated. It's the closest thing to having an AI assistant that follows you everywhere.
What the Ideal Sidebar Would Look Like
If I were designing from scratch, I'd steal from everyone:
┌─────┬───────────────────┬─────────────────────────┐
│Rail │ Expanded Panel    │ Main Content             │
│     │ (context-aware)   │                          │
│ 💬  │ ┌───────────────┐ │                          │
│ 📁  │ │ Recent Chats  │ │  Chat / Editor / Canvas  │
│ ⭐  │ │ ○ Chat 1  ... │ │                          │
│ 🔍  │ │ ○ Chat 2  ... │ │                          │
│ ⚙️  │ │ ○ Chat 3  ... │ │                          │
│     │ ├───────────────┤ │                          │
│     │ │ Projects      │ │                          │
│     │ │ 📁 Project A  │ │                          │
│     │ │ 📁 Project B  │ │                          │
│     │ ├───────────────┤ │                          │
│     │ │ Active Agents │ │                          │
│     │ │ ● Agent 1 🟢  │ │                          │
│     │ │ ● Agent 2 ⏳  │ │                          │
│     │ └───────────────┘ │                          │
│     │                   │                          │
└─────┴───────────────────┴─────────────────────────┘
Key principles:

Three states, not two. Rail (icons only, ~48px) → Expanded (~280px) → Hidden. The rail is always there unless you explicitly hide it.
The rail is a vertical tab bar. Icons for Chat History, Projects, Starred, Search, Settings. Click to expand that section. Like VS Code's Activity Bar, but for a chat product.
Context-aware expanded panel. When you click the chat icon on the rail, the expanded panel shows recent chats. When you click projects, it shows projects. When you click the active-agents icon, it shows running sessions with live status. One rail, many panels.
Rich list items. Each conversation in the list shows: title, model badge (small colored dot), relative date, message count, and attachment indicator. Not just a truncated title.
Keyboard-first. Ctrl+K opens a quick-switch fuzzy search across all conversations and projects. Arrow keys navigate the list. Enter opens. Delete removes.
Drag-and-drop. Drag a conversation into a project to organize it. Drag a conversation to the main area to open it as a tab (for multi-session workflows).
Multi-session tabs in the main area. Like browser tabs or VS Code editor tabs. You can have 2–3 conversations open as tabs and switch between them without losing scroll position or context.
Status indicators on the rail. A small badge on the agent icon showing how many agents are running. A dot on the chat icon if there are unread responses. Ambient awareness without needing to open anything.
Responsive collapse. On tablet, auto-collapse to rail. On mobile, collapse to hidden (swipe-in drawer). On desktop with a wide screen, default to expanded.
Resizable. Always. Drag the edge. Double-click the edge to snap between rail and expanded widths.

### VS Code-style Three-State Sidebar Architecture

**New files created:**
- ActivityRail.tsx — 48px vertical icon strip with Chat, DevLogs, Search, Settings icons. Spring-animated active indicator, engine status dot, VeggaAI logo as quick-switch trigger, conversation badge.

**Behavior:**

- Ctrl+S cycles: expanded → rail → hidden → expanded
- Ctrl+0 focus mode hides everything

User: I need away to view the knowledge base, can we add that to sidebar also and make that page updated and then I also wat to improve my sandbox ui and features + ui and currently my example is more like antigravity atm.. help me now look at this 'The Sandbox Preview Panel: Claude Artifacts vs Antigravity vs Base44

A deep dive into how each platform handles the "show me the thing I built" experience — the preview panel, code view, toolbar, hidden menus, responsive testing, and that Antigravity AI mouse trick.


Part 1: Claude's Artifacts Panel — The Gold Standard for Elegance
What It Is
When Claude generates something substantial — a React component, an HTML page, an SVG, a Mermaid diagram, markdown — it doesn't dump it inline in the chat. It opens a dedicated panel to the right of the conversation called the Artifacts panel. This is Claude's sandbox: a live rendering environment that sits beside your chat like a second monitor.
Panel Architecture
┌──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┐
│                      │  ┌────────────────────────┐  │
│  Chat conversation   │  │ [Title]     [▾ options]│  │
│                      │  │ ┌──────────┬─────────┐ │  │
│  User message        │  │ │ Preview  │  Code   │ │  │
│                      │  │ └──────────┴─────────┘ │  │
│  Claude response     │  │                        │  │
│  referencing the     │  │  ┌──────────────────┐  │  │
│  artifact...         │  │  │                  │  │  │
│                      │  │  │  LIVE PREVIEW    │  │  │
│                      │  │  │  (sandboxed      │  │  │
│                      │  │  │   iframe)        │  │  │
│                      │  │  │                  │  │  │
│                      │  │  │                  │  │  │
│                      │  │  └──────────────────┘  │  │
│                      │  │                        │  │
│                      │  │ [📋 Copy] [↗ Open]     │  │
│                      │  │ [⟳ Refresh] [💾 DL]    │  │
│                      │  └────────────────────────┘  │
│  [Input area]        │                              │
└──────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┘
The Two Modes — Preview vs Code
At the top of the artifact panel, there are two tab-like toggles:
Preview mode:

Renders the artifact live — HTML pages render as interactive webpages, React components run as functional apps, SVGs display as images, Mermaid diagrams render as flowcharts, markdown renders as formatted text.
The preview runs inside a sandboxed iframe — isolated from the main page. JavaScript executes, CSS applies, buttons are clickable, forms are fillable. It's a real browser environment.
You can interact with the preview: click buttons, type in inputs, toggle switches, scroll through content. It's not a screenshot — it's a living app.

Code mode:

Shows the raw source code with syntax highlighting.
Full syntax coloring per language (JSX, HTML, CSS, Python, etc.).
Line numbers along the left gutter.
Monospace font (similar to the code blocks in chat, but in a full-panel view).
The code is copyable — there's a dedicated copy button, or you can select-all and copy manually.
When Claude is actively editing the artifact, you can watch the inline diff updates — individual lines change in place rather than the whole file being rewritten (the 3-4x speed improvement introduced in late 2025).

The Toolbar — Every Button Explained
The artifact panel has a set of action buttons, some always visible, some in dropdown menus:
Always visible:

Title bar: Shows the artifact name (e.g., "Interactive Dashboard" or "Snake Game"). This is auto-generated or you can ask Claude to name it.
Preview / Code toggle: The two-tab switcher at the top. Clean, pill-shaped toggle. Active tab has a filled background; inactive is ghost/text-only.
Copy button (📋): Copies the entire source code to clipboard. Works in both Preview and Code mode. On click, briefly shows "Copied!" confirmation.
Download button: In the copy button dropdown (click the chevron), you get "Download as HTML" (or appropriate format). This saves the artifact as a standalone file.
Open in new tab / Expand (↗): Opens the preview in a full browser tab — no chat, no sidebar, just the rendered artifact at full viewport size. Great for testing responsive behavior or presenting to someone.
Refresh / Re-run (⟳): Re-renders the preview from the current code. Useful if you've been watching edits stream in and the preview got out of sync.

In the dropdown / overflow menu (⋯ or ▾):

Publish / Share: Generates a public URL for the artifact. Anyone with the link can view and interact with it. For AI-powered artifacts, users interact with Claude's intelligence through the artifact itself.
Remix: Lets other users fork your published artifact and modify it.
Version history: When Claude has edited the artifact multiple times in a conversation, you can browse previous versions and revert.
Add to project: Save the artifact to a Claude Project for organization.

Hidden Features You Don't See Immediately

Persistent storage API: Artifacts can use window.storage to read/write data that persists across sessions. This means you can build journals, trackers, and leaderboards that remember your data. Key-value store with personal and shared scopes.
Anthropic API access inside artifacts: React artifacts can make API calls to Claude itself — "Claude in Claude" / "Claudeception." The artifact can call fetch("https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages") without an API key (it's handled automatically). This means you can build AI-powered apps inside an artifact.
MCP server integration: On Pro/Max/Team/Enterprise plans, artifacts can connect to MCP servers (Gmail, Google Calendar, etc.) to build tools that interact with your real services.
Inline diff editing: Claude doesn't rewrite the entire artifact for small changes. It uses targeted string replacement — you can watch individual lines update in real-time during streaming. The system uses three distinct operations: "create," "update," and "rewrite."
Multiple artifact types: Not just code — Claude produces markdown (rendered as formatted docs), SVG (rendered as vector images), Mermaid (rendered as diagrams), PDF, and plain React/HTML. Each type gets appropriate rendering in Preview mode.
Library access: React artifacts can import from a curated set of libraries — Recharts, D3, Three.js, Plotly, Tone.js, TensorFlow.js, Chart.js, Lodash, MathJS, Papaparse, SheetJS, shadcn/ui, lucide-react icons, and more. These are pre-bundled and available without npm.
No localStorage: Browser storage APIs (localStorage, sessionStorage) are intentionally disabled in the sandbox. State must be managed via React state or the persistent storage API.

Styling Details

Panel background: White in light mode, dark gray (#1A1A1A) in dark mode.
Code syntax theme: Dark theme resembles VS Code's default dark, with colored tokens for keywords, strings, variables. Light theme uses a light editor style.
Toggle pills: Small, rounded, with ~2px border. Active state: filled accent color. Inactive: transparent with text.
Toolbar buttons: Ghost/icon buttons, ~32px touch target, subtle hover background. Muted color by default, darker on hover.
Panel border: Thin left border separating it from the chat area. Subtle shadow or 1px border in var(--border-primary).
Panel width: Roughly 50% of viewport on desktop. Not user-resizable via drag (the split is fixed). On narrow viewports, the panel takes over the full width and the chat slides away.
Transition: The panel slides in from the right when an artifact is first created. Smooth ~300ms animation.
Close behavior: You can close the artifact panel to go back to full-width chat. A small thumbnail or reference link remains in the chat message.

Mobile Behavior
On mobile, the artifact panel takes the full screen as a separate view. You tap on the artifact reference in the chat to open it, and use a back button/gesture to return to the conversation. The preview is still interactive and touch-friendly.

Part 2: Google Antigravity — The Browser-First Sandbox with AI Mouse Control
What It Is
Antigravity takes a fundamentally different approach. Instead of an iframe sandbox, it ships with a built-in Chromium browser as a first-class surface in the IDE. This is not a preview panel — it's a real browser where agents can navigate, click, type, screenshot, and verify their own work.
The Browser Panel
┌──────────┬───────────────────────┬────────────────────┐
│ Activity │  Editor Panel         │  Browser Panel     │
│ Bar      │  (code files)         │                    │
│          │                       │  [← → ⟳ URL bar ] │
│ 📁       │  app.tsx              │  ┌──────────────┐  │
│ 🔍       │  import React...      │  │              │  │
│ 🔀       │  function App() {     │  │  LIVE APP    │  │
│ 💬       │    return (           │  │  RENDERING   │  │
│ 🌐       │      <div>           │  │              │  │
│          │        ...            │  │  🖱️ AI cursor│  │
│          │      </div>           │  │  moving here │  │
│          │    )                   │  │              │  │
│          │  }                     │  │              │  │
│          │                       │  └──────────────┘  │
│          │                       │  [📸 Screenshot]   │
│          │                       │  [🖱️ Computer Use] │
└──────────┴───────────────────────┴────────────────────┘
The Killer Feature: AI Mouse Control (Computer Use)
This is what makes Antigravity genuinely different from everything else. Powered by Google's Gemini 2.5 Computer Use model, the agent doesn't just write code and hope it works — it:

Launches the app in the built-in browser.
Moves a visible cursor around the preview — you can literally watch a mouse pointer navigate the UI.
Clicks buttons, fills in forms, navigates between pages.
Takes screenshots at each step and analyzes them to verify the UI looks correct.
Records browser sessions as video-like artifact trails you can replay.
Identifies visual bugs — misaligned elements, broken layouts, missing content — by comparing what it sees to what it intended.

The AI mouse isn't a hidden feature — it's a core part of the workflow. When an agent finishes building a feature, it opens the browser, navigates to the relevant page, and tests it. You watch in real-time as the cursor moves, clicks, and types. It's like screen-sharing with an autonomous QA engineer.
Browser Panel Features

URL bar: Shows the local dev server URL (e.g., localhost:3000). You can navigate manually too.
Navigation controls: Back, forward, refresh — standard browser chrome.
Screenshot capture: The agent captures screenshots as verifiable artifacts. These appear in the Agent Manager as proof of work.
Browser recordings: Not just static screenshots — full interaction recordings showing the agent's mouse path, clicks, and page transitions.
Manual interaction: You can grab the mouse yourself and interact with the preview. It's a real Chromium instance.
Responsive testing: The browser panel is resizable, so you can test narrow/wide viewports by dragging the panel edge.

Artifact System (Different from Claude's)
Antigravity's artifacts are not live-rendered code previews — they're verifiable deliverables generated by agents:

Task lists: Checkbox-style breakdowns of what the agent plans to do.
Implementation plans: Detailed markdown documents explaining the approach.
Screenshots: Visual proof that the UI looks correct.
Browser recordings: Playable recordings of the agent interacting with the app.
Diffs/patches: Code changes with reasoning steps.

You can leave inline comments on any artifact — like commenting on a Google Doc — and the agent incorporates your feedback without stopping.
What's Not Visible at First Glance

Plan mode vs Fast mode: Plan mode generates a full plan artifact before acting (for complex tasks). Fast mode executes immediately (for quick fixes). The mode selector is in the agent panel, easy to miss.
Knowledge base: Antigravity saves useful context from conversations into a reusable knowledge base. It learns from your project over time.
Parallel agents: Multiple agents can work simultaneously on different tasks. Each has its own browser instance.
Agent feedback loop: Leave comments on artifacts mid-execution. The agent reads them without restarting.

Styling

VS Code DNA: The editor panel, Activity Bar, and file tree all look exactly like VS Code (because it's a fork). Same themes, same extensions.
Browser panel: Chrome-like frame with a minimal toolbar. White/dark background matching the theme.
Agent panel: Custom UI for mission control — card-based layout showing agent status, progress indicators, and artifact thumbnails.
Color scheme: Google's signature blue accents (#4285F4) mixed with VS Code's neutral palette.


Part 3: Base44 — The All-in-One Builder Preview
What It Is
Base44 is not an IDE — it's an AI app builder. Its preview system is designed for non-technical users who describe an app in plain English and see it materialize. The sandbox is built into the platform as the primary output surface.
Layout
┌───────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┐
│  Builder Chat         │  Live Preview                 │
│                       │                               │
│  [Discuss] [Build]    │  ┌─────────────────────────┐  │
│                       │  │                         │  │
│  "Build me a task     │  │   YOUR APP              │  │
│   tracker with        │  │   (fully interactive)   │  │
│   auth and..."        │  │                         │  │
│                       │  │   Login page             │  │
│  ┌─── Build Log ───┐  │  │   ┌──────────────┐     │  │
│  │ ✅ Generate schema│  │  │   │ Email: ____  │     │  │
│  │ ✅ Create routes  │  │  │   │ Pass:  ____  │     │  │
│  │ ✅ Build UI       │  │  │   │ [Login]      │     │  │
│  │ ⏳ Seed data     │  │  │   └──────────────┘     │  │
│  └──────────────────┘  │  │                         │  │
│                       │  └─────────────────────────┘  │
│  AI: "I've created    │                               │
│  your app with..."    │  [📱 Mobile] [🖥️ Desktop]     │
│                       │  [🔗 Share] [🚀 Deploy]       │
│  [Model: Auto ▾]      │  [</> Code]                   │
└───────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┘
Two Chat Modes
Base44's chat has a clever split:

Build mode: Your prompt actually triggers app generation/modification. Credits are consumed. Code is written, databases are created, UI is rendered.
Discussion mode: You chat with the AI architect without affecting the live app. No credits consumed. Great for brainstorming — "Should this be a modal or a separate page?" before committing.

The Preview Panel

Live and interactive: The preview is a fully running web app. You can click through pages, fill forms, test auth flows, interact with the database.
Instant deployment: Every build is immediately live — there's a shareable URL from the moment of creation.
Responsive toggles: Buttons to switch between mobile and desktop viewport sizes.
Real data: Unlike Claude's artifacts (which are stateless unless using the storage API), Base44 apps have a real database behind them. Data you enter in the preview persists.

Build Log
The left panel includes a real-time build log — a checklist that ticks through phases:

✅ Generate schema
✅ Create routes
✅ Build UI components
✅ Set up authentication
⏳ Seed sample data

This gives you visibility into what the AI is doing, similar to Antigravity's artifacts but more like a CI/CD pipeline view.
Code Access

View code: You can see the project's full codebase (React + Tailwind CSS).
Edit code: On paid plans, you can manually edit files.
Export / GitHub: Export the full codebase or push to a GitHub repo (beta).
The code view is secondary — Base44 is designed for people who don't want to touch code.

Model Picker
A dropdown lets you choose the AI model. By default it's "Auto" (the platform picks between Claude Sonnet 4 and Gemini 2.5 Pro). You can override this if you have a preference.
What You Don't See Immediately

Built-in database management: Every app gets an auto-generated database with schema, tables, and API endpoints. You can view and edit data directly in the platform.
Auth system: User authentication, roles, and permissions are built in — not bolted on.
Stripe integration: One-click payment/subscription setup.
Email system: Send transactional and marketing emails from within the app.
Analytics dashboard: Track user behavior and app performance without external tools.
Integration credits: A separate credit system for runtime operations (API calls, file uploads, emails).

Styling

Modern, clean, consumer-friendly: Rounded corners, soft shadows, generous whitespace. Feels more like Notion or Linear than VS Code.
Dark/light theme: Standard toggle.
Chat panel: Left side, ~40% width. Clean message bubbles with the build log integrated.
Preview panel: Right side, ~60% width. Full-height, full-fidelity rendering.
No Activity Bar, no file tree: This is not an IDE. The sidebar is just the chat.


Part 4: The Comparison — Claude vs Antigravity vs Base44
Feature Matrix
FeatureClaude ArtifactsAntigravity BrowserBase44 PreviewLive interactive preview✅ (sandboxed iframe)✅ (real Chromium)✅ (live deployed app)Code view with syntax highlighting✅✅ (full IDE editor)✅ (secondary)Copy code✅✅✅Download as file✅✅ (full repo)✅ (export)Open in new tab / full screen✅✅✅ (shareable URL)Refresh / re-render✅✅✅Publish / share publicly✅❌ (local IDE)✅ (instant deploy)AI mouse control / QA❌✅✅✅ (killer feature)❌Screenshot artifacts❌✅❌Browser recordings❌✅❌Real database behind preview❌ (in-memory only)✅ (dev server)✅✅ (production DB)Auth system built in❌❌ (you build it)✅✅Responsive viewport testingPartial (resize panel)✅ (resize browser)✅ (toggle buttons)Inline diff updates✅ (fast partial edits)✅ (standard IDE diffs)N/AVersion history✅ (per-conversation)✅ (Git)✅ (prompt history)Persistent storage✅ (storage API)✅ (filesystem)✅ (real database)AI-powered artifacts✅ (Claudeception)❌✅ (AI features in apps)MCP integration✅✅❌Library ecosystemCurated set (React, D3, etc.)Full npmFull npm via buildMobile previewFull-screen takeoverResizable panelToggle buttonComment on artifacts❌✅ (Google Docs style)Via chatMulti-agent parallel❌✅✅❌Build progress log❌ (streaming only)✅ (artifact trail)✅ (checklist)Target audienceEveryone (consumer)Developers (power users)Non-technical builders
My Verdict
Claude Artifacts: Best design, most accessible, most elegant.
Claude's panel is the best-designed sandbox for a consumer product. The Preview/Code toggle is clean and obvious. The sandbox is safe (no filesystem access, no network risks). The inline diff editing is fast. Publishing and sharing work with one click. The ability to build AI-powered artifacts (Claudeception) is unique and powerful. It's the only one that a non-developer can open, interact with, and share immediately.
Weaknesses: No real backend. No database persistence beyond the storage API. No responsive viewport toggles (you have to resize the panel manually). No AI-driven testing — Claude can't click around its own preview to verify it works.
Antigravity: Most powerful, most visionary, roughest edges.
The AI mouse control is genuinely groundbreaking. Watching an agent navigate your UI, click buttons, fill forms, and take screenshots to verify its own work is something nobody else offers. The built-in browser is a real Chromium instance, not a sandbox — it can test anything a user would do. The artifact system (plans, screenshots, recordings, comments) creates an auditable trail of the agent's reasoning.
Weaknesses: It's a local IDE, not a web product — you can't share a link to your preview. The UI can be overwhelming (it's VS Code + Agent Manager + Browser, all competing for screen space). It's in preview and has performance issues (UI freezes, rate limits). The browser panel requires the app to be running on a local dev server, which means more setup.
Base44: Most practical for shipping, least sophisticated sandbox.
Base44 is the only one where the preview is a real, deployed, production app from the moment it's created. The database is real. The auth is real. The URL is shareable. For non-developers who want to go from idea to live app, nothing is faster. The build log giving visibility into the AI's process is a nice touch.
Weaknesses: The preview is basic — it's just the running app in a frame. No code-level features (no syntax highlighting panel, no diff view, no version control). The code view is an afterthought. No AI QA or testing of any kind. You're testing manually, as a user. The platform is opinionated — React + Tailwind, no alternatives.
If I Could Combine Them
The dream sandbox would combine:

Claude's design elegance — the clean Preview/Code toggle, the one-click publish, the inline diff streaming, the curated library access.
Antigravity's AI mouse control — the agent testing its own work visually, taking screenshots, producing browser recordings as proof. This should exist in every AI builder.
Base44's production-readiness — real database, real auth, real deployment from moment one. The preview is the product.

Plus:
4. Responsive viewport presets — mobile/tablet/desktop toggle buttons (like browser DevTools). Claude doesn't have this. Base44 has basic toggles. Nobody has iPhone/Android frame previews.
5. Commenting on the preview — Antigravity's Google-Docs-style commenting, but on the live preview itself. Point at a button and say "make this bigger." The AI sees the comment's location and context.
6. Split preview — show mobile and desktop side by side. No tool does this well yet.
That combination — elegant consumer design + AI QA with visible mouse + real infrastructure + responsive testing + inline commenting — would be the definitive sandbox. Nobody has it yet. Claude is closest on design, Antigravity is closest on power, and Base44 is closest on practicality.' and I rly want mouse support for agents like  That visible AI cursor moving around your UI, clicking buttons, filling forms, and taking screenshots is genuinely unique. Completed: *Build verify* (6/6)

All done. Here's what was shipped:

### PreviewPanel — Claude Artifacts-style rewrite
- **Preview/Code pill toggle** — switch between live iframe preview and syntax-highlighted source code viewer
- **CodeView** with file tabs, line numbers, keyword coloring (imports violet, declarations blue, comments dimmed, strings emerald, returns amber), copy/download buttons
- **Enhanced toolbar**: URL bar with project name, responsive breakpoint group (mobile/tablet/desktop with violet active state), refresh, copy URL, screenshot, open external, destroy
- **Build step progress**: icon-based (CheckCircle/Loader2/XCircle) with labeled steps instead of anonymous colored bars
- **AI Cursor Overlay**: spring-animated violet cursor with "Vai" label, click ripple effect, exposed via `window.__vai_cursor` API (`moveTo`, `click`, `hide`, `screenshot`)
- **Screenshot flash** overlay for visual feedback
- **Animated transitions** (framer-motion) between Preview and Code views

### DebugConsole — Pro-grade upgrade
- **Line numbers** (toggleable via `#` button)
- **Timestamps** (toggleable via clock button) — tracks when each log line arrived
- **Search** — inline filter bar with match count indicator
- **Scroll lock** — amber lock icon to freeze auto-scroll while reading
- **ANSI strip** — cleans escape sequences for readable output
- **Error/warning badges** in header showing counts
- **Clear console** button
- All existing smart copy filters preserved

### FileExplorer — Syntax-highlighted viewer
- **File search** — filterable tree with match count
- **Syntax-highlighted viewer** with line numbers, keyword coloring, hover highlights
- **Copy + Download** buttons on viewer header
- **Line count** badge
- **Child count** badges on folders
- Violet selection highlight (was blue)
- Better empty states per search vs build status

### Keyboard shortcut
- **Ctrl+Shift+K** now opens the Knowledge Base panel

1. Architecture Document (vai-sandbox-architecture.md)
The full blueprint with seven parts:
Part 1 refines your sprawling idea into a clear system with named components and seven buildable phases — AI Cursor → Keyboard → Vision → Hover/Click Validation → Navigation → Radial Menu → Branch/Test/Merge pipeline.
Part 2 explains why each architectural decision was made (iframe + canvas overlay vs headless browser, why virtual keyboard, why radial vs dropdown, why branch-never-touch-template) — written so Vai learns the reasoning, not just the answer.
Part 3 gives the component specs — Preview Engine, Vai Agent Controller (full API surface), AI Cursor (with eased cubic interpolation), Virtual Keyboard (smart positioning that avoids occluding the focused input), Screenshot/Vision system (DOM ↔ visual text mapping — your "apple in code = apple on screen" idea), and the Radial Tool Menu (layered categories: Navigate, Validate, Edit, Helpers, SubVai, Tools).
Part 4 is Vai's Learning Framework — the seven Timeless Foundations: Reading Between the Lines, Asking the Right Question, Precision Communication, Systems Thinking, Pattern Recognition, Decomposition, and Verification Instinct. Each one has a specific practice method tied to what Vai does in the sandbox.
Part 5 defines SubVai workers — scoped, isolated, single-task workers that return results + learnings to Vai.
Part 6 is the phased build strategy (6 weeks) and recording recommendations (MediaRecorder API for short captures, WebSocket+FFmpeg for longer ones, always WebM VP9 at 1Mbps).
Part 7 contains the actual questions I'd ask Vai to develop each foundation.
2. Working React Prototype (vai-sandbox.jsx)
A Phase 0 artifact you can run right now. It includes:

Preview/Code toggle with syntax-highlighted code view + line numbers + copy button
Responsive viewport toggles (📱 mobile / 📟 tablet / 🖥️ desktop)
Animated AI cursor with eased cubic movement, element label, and indigo glow
Click ripple effect — expanding ring animation on click targets
Virtual keyboard overlay that appears during typing, with key-by-key highlighting, smart positioning, and typed-text preview
Radial tool menu — right-click the preview to see it. Six items (Navigate, Validate, Edit, Screenshot, SubVai, Tools) arranged in a circle with staggered pop-in animation
Action log panel showing every step Vai takes in real-time
Screenshot tracker counting captured states
Vai Learning section showing what foundation is being practiced
A full demo sequence — click "Run Vai Demo" and watch Vai navigate the template, hover over elements, click buttons, fill a form with the virtual keyboard, take screenshots, and report results' and 'import { useState, useRef, useEffect, useCallback } from "react";

// ─── Vai Sandbox: Phase 0 Prototype ───────────────────────────────
// AI Cursor + Virtual Keyboard + Preview/Code + Radial Menu Shell
// Built for Vetle & Vai — March 3, 2026

const DEMO_HTML = `<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<style>
  * { margin:0; padding:0; box-sizing:border-box; }
  body { font-family: system-ui, sans-serif; background: #0f1117; color: #e2e4e9; min-height:100vh; }
  .nav { display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:space-between; padding:16px 24px; border-bottom:1px solid #1e2028; background:#13151c; }
  .nav-brand { font-weight:700; font-size:18px; color:#a78bfa; }
  .nav-links { display:flex; gap:20px; }
  .nav-links a { color:#9ca3af; text-decoration:none; font-size:14px; transition:color 0.2s; }
  .nav-links a:hover { color:#e2e4e9; }
  .hero { padding:80px 24px; text-align:center; }
  .hero h1 { font-size:48px; font-weight:800; background:linear-gradient(135deg,#a78bfa,#6366f1,#3b82f6); -webkit-background-clip:text; -webkit-text-fill-color:transparent; margin-bottom:16px; }
  .hero p { color:#9ca3af; font-size:18px; max-width:560px; margin:0 auto 32px; line-height:1.6; }
  .btn { display:inline-block; padding:12px 28px; border-radius:10px; font-size:15px; font-weight:600; cursor:pointer; border:none; transition:all 0.2s; }
  .btn-primary { background:#6366f1; color:white; }
  .btn-primary:hover { background:#5558e6; transform:translateY(-1px); box-shadow:0 4px 20px rgba(99,102,241,0.4); }
  .btn-ghost { background:transparent; color:#9ca3af; border:1px solid #2a2d38; margin-left:12px; }
  .btn-ghost:hover { border-color:#6366f1; color:#e2e4e9; }
  .cards { display:grid; grid-template-columns:repeat(3,1fr); gap:20px; padding:40px 24px; max-width:900px; margin:0 auto; }
  .card { background:#1a1c25; border:1px solid #2a2d38; border-radius:14px; padding:28px; transition:all 0.3s; }
  .card:hover { border-color:#6366f1; transform:translateY(-3px); box-shadow:0 8px 30px rgba(0,0,0,0.3); }
  .card h3 { font-size:16px; margin-bottom:8px; color:#e2e4e9; }
  .card p { font-size:13px; color:#6b7280; line-height:1.5; }
  .form-section { max-width:400px; margin:40px auto; padding:32px; background:#1a1c25; border:1px solid #2a2d38; border-radius:14px; }
  .form-section h2 { font-size:20px; margin-bottom:20px; }
  .input { width:100%; padding:10px 14px; background:#0f1117; border:1px solid #2a2d38; border-radius:8px; color:#e2e4e9; font-size:14px; margin-bottom:12px; outline:none; transition:border-color 0.2s; }
  .input:focus { border-color:#6366f1; box-shadow:0 0 0 3px rgba(99,102,241,0.15); }
  .submit-btn { width:100%; padding:12px; background:#6366f1; color:white; border:none; border-radius:8px; font-size:15px; font-weight:600; cursor:pointer; transition:background 0.2s; }
  .submit-btn:hover { background:#5558e6; }
</style>
</head>
<body>
  <nav class="nav">
    <div class="nav-brand">VaiApp</div>
    <div class="nav-links">
      <a href="#">Features</a>
      <a href="#">Pricing</a>
      <a href="#">Docs</a>
      <a href="#">Contact</a>
    </div>
  </nav>
  <section class="hero">
    <h1>Build with Vai</h1>
    <p>An intelligent sandbox that sees, clicks, types, and validates. Let your AI agent test what it builds.</p>
    <button class="btn btn-primary" onclick="this.textContent='Clicked!'">Get Started</button>
    <button class="btn btn-ghost">Learn More</button>
  </section>
  <div class="cards">
    <div class="card"><h3>AI Cursor</h3><p>Watch Vai navigate your UI with a visible, animated cursor pointer.</p></div>
    <div class="card"><h3>Vision System</h3><p>Screenshot, compare, and validate visual states automatically.</p></div>
    <div class="card"><h3>Smart Keyboard</h3><p>Virtual keyboard appears contextually when Vai fills forms.</p></div>
  </div>
  <div class="form-section">
    <h2>Contact Vai</h2>
    <input class="input" type="text" placeholder="Your name" id="nameInput"/>
    <input class="input" type="email" placeholder="your@email.com" id="emailInput"/>
    <textarea class="input" placeholder="Your message..." rows="3" id="msgInput" style="resize:vertical"></textarea>
    <button class="submit-btn" onclick="this.textContent='Sent ✓'; this.style.background='#059669'">Send Message</button>
  </div>
</body>
</html>`;

// ─── Keyboard Layout ──────────────────────────────────────────────
const KB_ROWS = [
  ["Q","W","E","R","T","Y","U","I","O","P"],
  ["A","S","D","F","G","H","J","K","L"],
  ["Z","X","C","V","B","N","M","⌫"],
  ["Space","Enter"]
];

// ─── Radial Menu Items ────────────────────────────────────────────
const RADIAL_ITEMS = [
  { label: "Navigate", icon: "🧭", color: "#3b82f6" },
  { label: "Validate", icon: "✅", color: "#10b981" },
  { label: "Edit",     icon: "✏️", color: "#f59e0b" },
  { label: "Screenshot", icon: "📸", color: "#8b5cf6" },
  { label: "SubVai",   icon: "🤖", color: "#ec4899" },
  { label: "Tools",    icon: "🔧", color: "#6b7280" },
];

// ─── Main Component ───────────────────────────────────────────────
export default function VaiSandbox() {
  const [mode, setMode] = useState("preview");
  const [viewport, setViewport] = useState("desktop");
  const [cursorPos, setCursorPos] = useState({ x: -50, y: -50 });
  const [cursorVisible, setCursorVisible] = useState(false);
  const [cursorLabel, setCursorLabel] = useState("");
  const [kbVisible, setKbVisible] = useState(false);
  const [kbPosition, setKbPosition] = useState({ top: 0, left: 0 });
  const [activeKey, setActiveKey] = useState(null);
  const [typedText, setTypedText] = useState("");
  const [radialOpen, setRadialOpen] = useState(false);
  const [radialPos, setRadialPos] = useState({ x: 0, y: 0 });
  const [actionLog, setActionLog] = useState([]);
  const [isRunning, setIsRunning] = useState(false);
  const [currentStep, setCurrentStep] = useState("");
  const [screenshots, setScreenshots] = useState([]);
  const [clickEffect, setClickEffect] = useState(null);

  const iframeRef = useRef(null);
  const overlayRef = useRef(null);
  const animRef = useRef(null);

  const log = useCallback((msg) => {
    setActionLog(prev => [...prev.slice(-30), { time: Date.now(), msg }]);
  }, []);

  // ─── Cursor Animation ────────────────────────────────────
  const animateCursorTo = useCallback((tx, ty, duration = 500) => {
    return new Promise(resolve => {
      const sx = cursorPos.x, sy = cursorPos.y;
      const start = performance.now();
      setCursorVisible(true);

      function frame(now) {
        const t = Math.min((now - start) / duration, 1);
        const ease = t < 0.5 ? 4*t*t*t : 1 - Math.pow(-2*t+2,3)/2;
        const nx = sx + (tx - sx) * ease;
        const ny = sy + (ty - sy) * ease;
        setCursorPos({ x: nx, y: ny });
        if (t < 1) animRef.current = requestAnimationFrame(frame);
        else resolve();
      }
      if (animRef.current) cancelAnimationFrame(animRef.current);
      animRef.current = requestAnimationFrame(frame);
    });
  }, [cursorPos]);

  // ─── Click Effect ────────────────────────────────────────
  const showClickEffect = useCallback((x, y) => {
    setClickEffect({ x, y, id: Date.now() });
    setTimeout(() => setClickEffect(null), 600);
  }, []);

  // ─── Type Animation ──────────────────────────────────────
  const typeText = useCallback(async (text) => {
    setKbVisible(true);
    setTypedText("");
    for (let i = 0; i < text.length; i++) {
      const ch = text[i];
      const key = ch === " " ? "Space" : ch.toUpperCase();
      setActiveKey(key);
      setTypedText(prev => prev + ch);
      await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 60 + Math.random() * 40));
      setActiveKey(null);
      await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 20));
    }
    setKbVisible(false);
  }, []);

  // ─── Demo Sequence ───────────────────────────────────────
  const runDemo = useCallback(async () => {
    if (isRunning) return;
    setIsRunning(true);
    setActionLog([]);
    setScreenshots([]);

    const wait = ms => new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, ms));
    const step = async (label, fn) => {
      setCurrentStep(label);
      log(`▸ ${label}`);
      await fn();
      await wait(300);
    };

    // Move to nav brand
    await step("Moving to navbar brand", async () => {
      await animateCursorTo(80, 30, 600);
      setCursorLabel("nav-brand");
    });

    await step("Hovering over 'Features' link", async () => {
      await animateCursorTo(420, 30, 500);
      setCursorLabel("Features");
      await wait(400);
    });

    await step("Clicking 'Get Started' button", async () => {
      await animateCursorTo(340, 260, 700);
      setCursorLabel("btn-primary");
      showClickEffect(340, 260);
      await wait(200);
      log("  ✓ Button click dispatched");
    });

    await step("📸 Taking screenshot", async () => {
      setScreenshots(prev => [...prev, { id: Date.now(), label: "After hero click" }]);
      log("  ✓ Screenshot captured");
    });

    await step("Inspecting card: 'AI Cursor'", async () => {
      await animateCursorTo(200, 410, 600);
      setCursorLabel("card[0]");
      await wait(500);
      log("  ✓ Hover state detected (border-color change)");
    });

    await step("Navigating to form section", async () => {
      await animateCursorTo(340, 540, 800);
      setCursorLabel("form-section");
    });

    // Focus name input and type
    await step("Focusing name input", async () => {
      const inputY = 580;
      await animateCursorTo(340, inputY, 500);
      setCursorLabel("input#nameInput");
      showClickEffect(340, inputY);
      setKbPosition({ top: inputY + 50, left: 100 });
      await wait(200);
    });

    await step("Typing name: 'Vetle'", async () => {
      await typeText("Vetle");
      log("  ✓ Typed 'Vetle' into name field");
    });

    // Focus email input
    await step("Focusing email input", async () => {
      const inputY = 620;
      await animateCursorTo(340, inputY, 400);
      setCursorLabel("input#emailInput");
      showClickEffect(340, inputY);
      setKbPosition({ top: inputY + 50, left: 100 });
      await wait(200);
    });

    await step("Typing email: 'vetle@vai.dev'", async () => {
      await typeText("vetle@vai.dev");
      log("  ✓ Typed email into field");
    });

    await step("Clicking 'Send Message' button", async () => {
      await animateCursorTo(340, 720, 500);
      setCursorLabel("submit-btn");
      showClickEffect(340, 720);
      await wait(200);
      log("  ✓ Form submitted");
    });

    await step("📸 Final screenshot", async () => {
      setScreenshots(prev => [...prev, { id: Date.now(), label: "Form submitted" }]);
      log("  ✓ Final state captured");
    });

    await step("✅ Validation complete", async () => {
      log("  All interactive elements tested");
      log("  2 screenshots captured");
      log("  0 errors detected");
    });

    setCursorVisible(false);
    setCursorLabel("");
    setCurrentStep("Demo complete");
    setIsRunning(false);
  }, [isRunning, animateCursorTo, typeText, showClickEffect, log]);

  // ─── Radial Menu Handler ────────────────────────────────
  const handleOverlayContextMenu = (e) => {
    e.preventDefault();
    const rect = overlayRef.current?.getBoundingClientRect();
    if (rect) {
      setRadialPos({ x: e.clientX - rect.left, y: e.clientY - rect.top });
      setRadialOpen(true);
    }
  };

  // Close radial on any click
  useEffect(() => {
    if (radialOpen) {
      const close = () => setRadialOpen(false);
      window.addEventListener("click", close);
      return () => window.removeEventListener("click", close);
    }
  }, [radialOpen]);

  const viewportWidth = viewport === "mobile" ? "375px" : viewport === "tablet" ? "768px" : "100%";

  return (
    <div style={{ display:"flex", flexDirection:"column", height:"100vh", background:"#0a0b0f", color:"#e2e4e9", fontFamily:"'SF Mono','Fira Code','JetBrains Mono',monospace" }}>

      {/* ─── Top Toolbar ─────────────────────────── */}
      <div style={{ display:"flex", alignItems:"center", justifyContent:"space-between", padding:"10px 16px", background:"#101218", borderBottom:"1px solid #1e2028", flexShrink:0, gap:12, flexWrap:"wrap" }}>
        <div style={{ display:"flex", alignItems:"center", gap:12 }}>
          <span style={{ fontWeight:800, fontSize:15, background:"linear-gradient(135deg,#a78bfa,#6366f1)", WebkitBackgroundClip:"text", WebkitTextFillColor:"transparent" }}>Vai Sandbox</span>
          <span style={{ fontSize:11, color:"#4b5563", padding:"2px 8px", border:"1px solid #1e2028", borderRadius:4 }}>Phase 0</span>
        </div>

        {/* Mode Toggle */}
        <div style={{ display:"flex", background:"#1a1c25", borderRadius:8, overflow:"hidden", border:"1px solid #2a2d38" }}>
          {["preview","code"].map(m => (
            <button key={m} onClick={() => setMode(m)} style={{
              padding:"6px 16px", fontSize:12, fontWeight:600, fontFamily:"inherit",
              background: mode===m ? "#6366f1" : "transparent",
              color: mode===m ? "#fff" : "#6b7280",
              border:"none", cursor:"pointer", textTransform:"capitalize", transition:"all 0.15s"
            }}>{m}</button>
          ))}
        </div>

        {/* Viewport Toggle */}
        <div style={{ display:"flex", gap:4 }}>
          {[{v:"mobile",icon:"📱"},{v:"tablet",icon:"📟"},{v:"desktop",icon:"🖥️"}].map(({v,icon}) => (
            <button key={v} onClick={() => setViewport(v)} style={{
              padding:"4px 10px", fontSize:14, borderRadius:6,
              background: viewport===v ? "#1e2028" : "transparent",
              border: viewport===v ? "1px solid #6366f1" : "1px solid transparent",
              cursor:"pointer", color:"#e2e4e9", transition:"all 0.15s"
            }}>{icon}</button>
          ))}
        </div>

        {/* Action Buttons */}
        <div style={{ display:"flex", gap:8 }}>
          <button onClick={runDemo} disabled={isRunning} style={{
            padding:"6px 16px", fontSize:12, fontWeight:700, borderRadius:8, fontFamily:"inherit",
            background: isRunning ? "#374151" : "linear-gradient(135deg,#6366f1,#8b5cf6)",
            color:"#fff", border:"none", cursor: isRunning?"not-allowed":"pointer",
            boxShadow: isRunning ? "none" : "0 2px 12px rgba(99,102,241,0.3)", transition:"all 0.2s"
          }}>{isRunning ? "⏳ Running..." : "▶ Run Vai Demo"}</button>
          <button style={{ padding:"6px 12px", fontSize:13, borderRadius:8, background:"transparent", border:"1px solid #2a2d38", color:"#9ca3af", cursor:"pointer", fontFamily:"inherit" }}>📸</button>
          <button style={{ padding:"6px 12px", fontSize:13, borderRadius:8, background:"transparent", border:"1px solid #2a2d38", color:"#9ca3af", cursor:"pointer", fontFamily:"inherit" }}>⟳</button>
        </div>
      </div>

      {/* ─── Main Content ────────────────────────── */}
      <div style={{ display:"flex", flex:1, overflow:"hidden" }}>

        {/* ─── Preview / Code Panel ──────────────── */}
        <div style={{ flex:1, position:"relative", overflow:"hidden", display:"flex", justifyContent:"center", background:"#0d0e14" }}>

          {mode === "preview" ? (
            <div ref={overlayRef} onContextMenu={handleOverlayContextMenu}
              style={{ position:"relative", width:viewportWidth, maxWidth:"100%", height:"100%", transition:"width 0.3s ease", margin:"0 auto" }}>

              {/* Iframe */}
              <iframe ref={iframeRef} srcDoc={DEMO_HTML} title="Preview" sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin"
                style={{ width:"100%", height:"100%", border:"none", display:"block" }} />

              {/* AI Cursor Layer */}
              {cursorVisible && (
                <div style={{ position:"absolute", top:0, left:0, width:"100%", height:"100%", pointerEvents:"none", zIndex:10 }}>
                  {/* Cursor pointer */}
                  <svg width="24" height="28" style={{ position:"absolute", left:cursorPos.x, top:cursorPos.y, filter:"drop-shadow(0 2px 8px rgba(99,102,241,0.5))", transition:"none" }} viewBox="0 0 24 28">
                    <path d="M1 1L1 21L6.5 16L12 26L16 24L10 14L17 14Z" fill="#6366f1" stroke="#fff" strokeWidth="1.5" strokeLinejoin="round"/>
                  </svg>
                  {/* Label */}
                  {cursorLabel && (
                    <div style={{ position:"absolute", left:cursorPos.x+22, top:cursorPos.y+8, background:"rgba(99,102,241,0.9)", color:"#fff", fontSize:10, fontWeight:600, padding:"2px 8px", borderRadius:4, whiteSpace:"nowrap", fontFamily:"inherit" }}>{cursorLabel}</div>
                  )}
                </div>
              )}

              {/* Click Ripple Effect */}
              {clickEffect && (
                <div key={clickEffect.id} style={{ position:"absolute", left:clickEffect.x-20, top:clickEffect.y-20, width:40, height:40, borderRadius:"50%", border:"2px solid #6366f1", pointerEvents:"none", zIndex:11, animation:"clickRipple 0.6s ease-out forwards" }} />
              )}

              {/* Virtual Keyboard */}
              {kbVisible && (
                <div style={{
                  position:"absolute", left:"50%", transform:"translateX(-50%)",
                  top: kbPosition.top, zIndex:20,
                  background:"rgba(16,18,24,0.95)", backdropFilter:"blur(12px)",
                  border:"1px solid #2a2d38", borderRadius:12,
                  padding:"10px 8px", boxShadow:"0 8px 40px rgba(0,0,0,0.5)",
                  display:"flex", flexDirection:"column", gap:6, alignItems:"center"
                }}>
                  {/* Typed text preview */}
                  <div style={{ fontSize:11, color:"#6366f1", fontWeight:600, padding:"2px 10px", marginBottom:2, fontFamily:"inherit" }}>
                    {typedText}<span style={{ animation:"blink 1s infinite", color:"#6366f1" }}>│</span>
                  </div>
                  {KB_ROWS.map((row, ri) => (
                    <div key={ri} style={{ display:"flex", gap:4, justifyContent:"center" }}>
                      {row.map(key => {
                        const isActive = activeKey === key;
                        const isWide = key === "Space" || key === "Enter";
                        return (
                          <div key={key} style={{
                            width: isWide ? (key==="Space" ? 160 : 80) : 30,
                            height: 30, display:"flex", alignItems:"center", justifyContent:"center",
                            borderRadius: 6, fontSize: isWide ? 10 : 11, fontWeight: 600,
                            fontFamily:"inherit",
                            background: isActive ? "#6366f1" : "#1a1c25",
                            color: isActive ? "#fff" : "#9ca3af",
                            border: `1px solid ${isActive ? "#818cf8" : "#2a2d38"}`,
                            transform: isActive ? "scale(0.92)" : "none",
                            transition: "all 0.08s ease",
                            boxShadow: isActive ? "0 0 12px rgba(99,102,241,0.4)" : "none",
                          }}>{key === "Space" ? "───────" : key}</div>
                        );
                      })}
                    </div>
                  ))}
                </div>
              )}

              {/* Radial Menu */}
              {radialOpen && (
                <div style={{ position:"absolute", left:radialPos.x, top:radialPos.y, zIndex:30, pointerEvents:"none" }}>
                  {RADIAL_ITEMS.map((item, i) => {
                    const angle = (i / RADIAL_ITEMS.length) * Math.PI * 2 - Math.PI / 2;
                    const radius = 72;
                    const ix = Math.cos(angle) * radius;
                    const iy = Math.sin(angle) * radius;
                    return (
                      <div key={i} style={{
                        position:"absolute", left:ix-28, top:iy-28,
                        width:56, height:56, borderRadius:"50%",
                        background:"rgba(16,18,24,0.95)", border:`2px solid ${item.color}`,
                        display:"flex", flexDirection:"column", alignItems:"center", justifyContent:"center",
                        pointerEvents:"all", cursor:"pointer",
                        boxShadow:`0 0 16px ${item.color}33`,
                        animation:`radialPop 0.25s ease-out ${i * 0.04}s both`,
                        fontSize:11, fontWeight:600, color:item.color
                      }}>
                        <span style={{ fontSize:18 }}>{item.icon}</span>
                        <span style={{ fontSize:7, marginTop:1 }}>{item.label}</span>
                      </div>
                    );
                  })}
                  {/* Center dot */}
                  <div style={{ position:"absolute", left:-6, top:-6, width:12, height:12, borderRadius:"50%", background:"#6366f1", border:"2px solid #818cf8" }} />
                </div>
              )}
            </div>
          ) : (
            /* ─── Code View ─────────────────────── */
            <div style={{ width:"100%", height:"100%", overflow:"auto", padding:16 }}>
              <div style={{ display:"flex", justifyContent:"space-between", alignItems:"center", marginBottom:12 }}>
                <span style={{ fontSize:12, color:"#6b7280" }}>template.html</span>
                <button onClick={() => { navigator.clipboard.writeText(DEMO_HTML); }}
                  style={{ padding:"4px 12px", fontSize:11, background:"#1a1c25", border:"1px solid #2a2d38", borderRadius:6, color:"#9ca3af", cursor:"pointer", fontFamily:"inherit" }}>
                  📋 Copy
                </button>
              </div>
              <pre style={{ fontSize:12, lineHeight:1.7, color:"#d1d5db", whiteSpace:"pre-wrap", wordBreak:"break-all" }}>
                {DEMO_HTML.split("\n").map((line, i) => (
                  <div key={i} style={{ display:"flex" }}>
                    <span style={{ width:44, textAlign:"right", paddingRight:16, color:"#374151", userSelect:"none", flexShrink:0 }}>{i+1}</span>
                    <span>{colorize(line)}</span>
                  </div>
                ))}
              </pre>
            </div>
          )}
        </div>

        {/* ─── Action Log Panel ──────────────────── */}
        <div style={{ width:280, flexShrink:0, borderLeft:"1px solid #1e2028", background:"#101218", display:"flex", flexDirection:"column", overflow:"hidden" }}>
          {/* Status */}
          <div style={{ padding:"12px 14px", borderBottom:"1px solid #1e2028" }}>
            <div style={{ fontSize:11, fontWeight:700, color:"#6366f1", textTransform:"uppercase", letterSpacing:1, marginBottom:6 }}>Vai Status</div>
            <div style={{ fontSize:12, color: isRunning ? "#fbbf24" : "#10b981", display:"flex", alignItems:"center", gap:6 }}>
              <span style={{ width:6, height:6, borderRadius:"50%", background: isRunning ? "#fbbf24" : "#10b981", display:"inline-block", boxShadow:`0 0 8px ${isRunning ? "#fbbf2466" : "#10b98166"}` }} />
              {isRunning ? currentStep : "Idle — Ready"}
            </div>
          </div>

          {/* Log */}
          <div style={{ flex:1, overflow:"auto", padding:"8px 14px" }}>
            <div style={{ fontSize:10, fontWeight:700, color:"#4b5563", textTransform:"uppercase", letterSpacing:1, marginBottom:8 }}>Action Log</div>
            {actionLog.length === 0 && (
              <div style={{ fontSize:11, color:"#374151", fontStyle:"italic" }}>Run the demo to see Vai in action...</div>
            )}
            {actionLog.map((entry, i) => (
              <div key={i} style={{ fontSize:11, color: entry.msg.startsWith("  ✓") ? "#10b981" : "#9ca3af", marginBottom:4, lineHeight:1.4 }}>
                {entry.msg}
              </div>
            ))}
          </div>

          {/* Screenshots */}
          {screenshots.length > 0 && (
            <div style={{ borderTop:"1px solid #1e2028", padding:"10px 14px" }}>
              <div style={{ fontSize:10, fontWeight:700, color:"#4b5563", textTransform:"uppercase", letterSpacing:1, marginBottom:6 }}>Screenshots ({screenshots.length})</div>
              {screenshots.map(s => (
                <div key={s.id} style={{ fontSize:11, color:"#8b5cf6", marginBottom:3 }}>📸 {s.label}</div>
              ))}
            </div>
          )}

          {/* Vai Learning Note */}
          <div style={{ borderTop:"1px solid #1e2028", padding:"10px 14px", background:"#0f1015" }}>
            <div style={{ fontSize:10, fontWeight:700, color:"#6366f1", textTransform:"uppercase", letterSpacing:1, marginBottom:4 }}>🧠 Vai Learning</div>
            <div style={{ fontSize:10, color:"#6b7280", lineHeight:1.5 }}>
              {isRunning ? "Observing: cursor movement, element targeting, form interaction patterns..."
                : "Foundations: decomposition, verification instinct, precision communication. Right-click preview for radial tool menu."}
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>

      {/* CSS Animations */}
      <style>{`
        @keyframes clickRipple {
          0% { transform: scale(0.5); opacity: 1; }
          100% { transform: scale(2.5); opacity: 0; }
        }
        @keyframes blink {
          0%, 50% { opacity: 1; }
          51%, 100% { opacity: 0; }
        }
        @keyframes radialPop {
          0% { transform: scale(0); opacity: 0; }
          100% { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; }
        }
      `}</style>
    </div>
  );
}

// ─── Basic Syntax Colorizer ───────────────────────────────────────
function colorize(line) {
  return line
    .replace(/(&lt;[\/]?\w+)/g, '<span style="color:#f472b6">$1</span>')
    .replace(/(class|style|id|type|placeholder|onclick|href|rows|src)=/g, '<span style="color:#38bdf8">$1</span>=')
    .replace(/"([^"]*)"/g, '"<span style="color:#a78bfa">$1</span>"')
    .replace(/(\/\/.+)$/g, '<span style="color:#4b5563">$1</span>');
}
'

Completed: *Build verify zero errors* (5/5)

Here's what was fixed:

**Bug 1 — Chat Enter/Send broken:**
- **Root cause:** `fetchModels` was only called when the Settings sidebar panel mounted. If the sidebar wasn't on Settings, `selectedModelId` stayed `null` and `handleSend` silently returned.
- **Fix:** Added `fetchModels()` call in App.tsx on startup so models load regardless of sidebar state. Added a `toast.error()` in ChatWindow.tsx if somehow no model is available — no more silent failures.

**Bug 2 — Sandbox toggle buttons badly positioned:**
- **Removed** the floating `PanelControls` overlay from App.tsx that was `absolute right-3 top-2` covering chat content.
- **Builder toggle + Focus mode** moved to the ActivityRail.tsx bottom section — consistent with the rail-based navigation.
- **Console + File explorer toggles** moved into the PreviewPanel.tsx with a separator — they're sandbox-specific and now live where they belong.

**Bug 3 — Code tab visible when no project has files:**
- The Code button in PreviewPanel.tsx is now conditionally rendered only when `hasFiles` is true.
- Auto-resets `viewMode` to `'preview'` if files disappear while on the Code tab.

Build: **2226 modules, zero errors.** Phase 0 sandbox integration is tracked but not started — let me know if you want to proceed with that.

Made changes.

Ran terminal command: cd c:\Users\v3gga\Documents\dev-vai; node scripts/session-bridge.mjs push ses_1772452307507_i0wqia note "Phase 0 Vai Sandbox integration complete: VaiCursor (eased cubic interpolation, trail, hover glow, typing indicator), VirtualKeyboard (QWERTY overlay, smart positioning, key press animation), RadialMenu (6 categories: Navigate/Validate/Edit/Screenshot/SubVai/Tools, ring layout with staggered pop), ActionLog (collapsible feed, auto-scroll, screenshot counter), expanded __vai_cursor API (moveTo, click, hover, focus, type, scroll, navigateTo, screenshot, openRadialMenu, selectRadialItem, assertVisible, assertText, log, getState). Build: 2230 modules, zero errors."

**Phase 0 Vai Sandbox integration complete.** Build: **2230 modules, zero errors, zero lint issues.** 

Here's what was built:

### New Components (in apps/desktop/src/components/sandbox/)

1. **VaiCursor.tsx** — Eased cubic interpolation cursor (not spring — intentional per architecture spec). Trail afterimage on fast moves, hover glow ring, click ripple, typing indicator dot, "Vai" label badge.

2. **VirtualKeyboard.tsx** — QWERTY overlay with smart viewport-aware positioning. Keys highlight and scale on press. Appears when Vai is typing into a form field.

3. **RadialMenu.tsx** — 6-category ring (Navigate, Validate, Edit, Screenshot, SubVai, Tools) with staggered pop animation, backdrop blur, center hub label, and active glow per item.

4. **ActionLog.tsx** — Collapsible action feed anchored bottom-left, auto-scrolling, color-coded per action type, screenshot counter badge, timestamps.

### Expanded `window.__vai_cursor` API

Full agent controller surface from the architecture spec:
- **Cursor**: `moveTo`, `click`, `hover`, `focus` (with label), `hide`
- **Typing**: `type(x, y, text)` — animates virtual keyboard key-by-key
- **Navigation**: `scroll`, `navigateTo`, `screenshot`
- **Radial**: `openRadialMenu`, `closeRadialMenu`, `selectRadialItem`
- **Validation**: `assertVisible`, `assertText`
- **Debug**: `log`, `getState`

Made changes.

Completed: *Build verify demo* (12/12)

Everything is complete. Here's the full summary of what was built across this session:

### Files Created
| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| sandbox/VaiCursor.tsx | Eased cubic interpolation AI cursor with trail, hover glow, click ripple, typing indicator |
| sandbox/VirtualKeyboard.tsx | QWERTY overlay with smart positioning, key press animation |
| sandbox/RadialMenu.tsx | 6-category ring menu (Navigate/Validate/Edit/Screenshot/SubVai/Tools) |
| sandbox/ActionLog.tsx | Collapsible action feed with auto-scroll, color-coded types, screenshot counter |
| sandbox/DemoSequence.ts | Declarative demo runner with default choreographed sequence |

### Files Modified
| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| PreviewPanel.tsx | Replaced old AICursorOverlay with full Phase 0 overlay stack; expanded `window.__vai_cursor` API (16 methods); added demo play/stop button in toolbar; exposed `window.__vai_demo` |
| App.tsx | Added `fetchModels()` on startup; removed floating PanelControls |
| ChatWindow.tsx | Added toast feedback when no model selected (fixes silent send failure) |
| ActivityRail.tsx | Added builder toggle + focus mode buttons at bottom |

### How to Test
- **Demo sequence**: Deploy any sandbox project, then click the **Play** button (violet ▶) in the PreviewPanel toolbar — watch the cursor move, type, open radial menu, take screenshots
- **Console API**: Open browser devtools and call `window.__vai_cursor.click(200, 300)`, `window.__vai_cursor.type(200, 300, "hello")`, `window.__vai_cursor.openRadialMenu(400, 300)`, or `window.__vai_demo.run()`
- **Build**: 2231 modules, zero errors
